lensOut.lens: PWA + mobile-app packaging engineer — "what must the PWA do NOW to ship as a fast mobile app, one codebase"
lensOut.summary: VenuePro's dashboard/admin Livewire PWA can be released as a Play Store + App Store app in days (not a rewrite) IF the app-shell, manifest, deep-link/asset-link verification files, push subscription model, and session storage are designed app-ready from v1 — all served from one Laravel origin so a TWA (Bubblewrap) and an iOS Capacitor/PWABuilder wrapper just point at the same URLs. The two real risks are wrapping-hostile auth (Livewire's session-cookie + CSRF + POST /livewire/update must survive WebView/Custom-Tabs and never be cached by the service worker) and Apple's 4.2 "minimum functionality" gate (a thin web-wrapper gets rejected). Everything below keeps it ONE codebase: the web PWA is the product; TWA/Capacitor are thin shells over the same origin, with native-only deltas (FCM/APNs push, secure storage, status-bar) behind capability detection.

requirements.requirement: Structure the Livewire dashboard/admin as a persistent app-shell SPA (static top bar, bottom tab nav, content region) driven by wire:navigate, so the chrome survives wrapping and feels native — no full-page reloads inside the shell. Public SSR pages stay multi-page for SEO; the two surfaces share design tokens but are separately routed.
requirements.how: Use a dedicated app-shell Blade layout for all dashboard/admin routes; add wire:navigate to every internal nav link so Livewire's SPA navigation keeps the shell mounted and only swaps the body. Reserve fixed safe-area-padded top/bottom bars (env(safe-area-inset-*)) and add viewport-fit=cover + apple-mobile-web-app-status-bar-style so notches/home-indicator render correctly when wrapped. Persist scroll/state per tab. Keep the shell's HTML+critical CSP-nonced CSS precached by the service worker so cold-start in the wrapper paints instantly.

requirements.requirement: Ship a complete, installable manifest now with the exact fields Bubblewrap/PWABuilder read to generate the native projects, so the app's name, icon, color, and start behaviour are correct on day one of packaging.
requirements.how: Serve /manifest.webmanifest (correct MIME, same origin) with: name, short_name, id (stable, e.g. "/?source=pwa"), start_url ("/dashboard?source=twa" so installs are attributable), scope "/", display "standalone", display_override ["standalone","minimal-ui"], orientation portrait, theme_color + background_color matching the design-system brand, lang "id", dir ltr, categories ["sports","business"]. Include icons at 192/512 plus a dedicated 512 maskable icon (purpose "maskable", brand-safe-zone padded) and a monochrome icon for notification badges. Add shortcuts (Bookings, Calendar, Top-up) and screenshots (narrow + wide form_factor) so both stores and the install UI show rich previews. Validate with PWABuilder + Lighthouse PWA/installability checks in CI so the manifest can't silently regress (ties to the Lighthouse ≥99 gate).

requirements.requirement: Make the origin TWA-ready now: host the Digital Asset Links file so a Bubblewrap build verifies instantly and the URL bar is hidden (a TWA that fails DAL verification shows a browser chrome and fails review).
requirements.how: Serve a route /.well-known/assetlinks.json from Laravel (NOT a SW-cached static the wrapper might stale) returning the relation delegate_permission/common.handle_all_urls with the app package name (e.g. asia.venuepro.app) and the Play App Signing SHA-256 fingerprint. Because Google re-signs via Play App Signing, take the SHA-256 from the Play Console (App integrity) — not the local upload key — and support an array of fingerprints (upload + Play) to avoid verification breakage. Keep a checked-in twa-manifest.json / Bubblewrap config (host, name, themeColor, backgroundColor, startUrl, iconUrl pointing at the 512 maskable) so `bubblewrap init/update/build` is reproducible in CI. Document the May-2026 Play closed-testing requirement (12 testers / 14 days for new personal accounts) so launch timeline accounts for it.

requirements.requirement: Choose and prepare the iOS wrapper path now (Capacitor preferred over a pure PWABuilder WKWebView) and design every screen to clear Apple's 4.2 minimum-functionality bar, because a thin web-wrapper of a public site is the most common rejection.
requirements.how: Default to a Capacitor iOS project that loads the same origin but adds genuine native value to pass 4.2: native push (APNs via Capacitor Push/FCM), biometric unlock of the dashboard, native share/add-to-calendar, haptics, and offline app-shell. Keep all of this behind capability detection (window.Capacitor?.isNativePlatform()) so the identical bundle runs as web PWA and inside the shell — one codebase. Host /.well-known/apple-app-site-association (served as application/json, no extension, same origin, with the Team ID + bundle ID appLinks paths) NOW so Universal Links resolve when the iOS app ships. Avoid relying on iOS home-screen-PWA push as the iOS strategy (fragile, install-gated); use it only as a no-app fallback.

requirements.requirement: Define a stable, app-mappable URL scheme now so the same https links open the right screen in browser, TWA, and iOS app without redesign — booking/venue/dashboard deep links must round-trip.
requirements.how: Keep canonical https paths as the single deep-link surface (no custom myapp:// scheme as the primary): /venue/{slug}, /{sport}/{city}/{slug}, /dashboard/bookings/{id}, /pay/{ref}. TWA handles all in-scope URLs automatically via DAL; for out-of-app links add an Android intent-filter with android:autoVerify="true" matching the host so App Links open the app directly. On iOS list the same paths in the AASA appLinks. Ensure server routes render a valid standalone page for every deep-linkable URL (so a cold app-open deep link doesn't 404), and carry a notification deep_link URL in every push payload.

requirements.requirement: Design the push subscription + delivery layer now so adding native FCM/APNs later is a token-source swap, not a backend redesign — reuse it for the existing WhatsApp/email reminder pipeline.
requirements.how: Add a push_subscriptions table (user_id, platform [webpush|fcm|apns], endpoint/token, keys, ua, last_seen) and a provider-agnostic Notification channel so the booking-confirmation/reminder/low-credit-balance events already fan out to push exactly as they do to WA/email (extends the spec's notifications_log + queued model). For web push now: VAPID keys, SW push+notificationclick handlers (notificationclick opens the deep link via clients.openWindow), and a permission prompt fired ONLY from a user gesture (required on iOS installed PWAs, 16.4+). For native later: Capacitor/Bubblewrap deliver an FCM token (Android) and APNs token (iOS) into the same table — no business-logic change. Store a VAPID/FCM server key server-side only.

requirements.area: Secure session & storage in the wrapper
requirements.requirement: Guarantee Livewire's session-cookie + CSRF auth survives both the TWA (Chrome Custom Tabs) and Capacitor (WKWebView) containers and that no auth/POST response is ever cached or leaked — this is the #1 thing that silently breaks when wrapping a Livewire app.
requirements.how: Set session/auth cookies Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax (NOT Strict — Lax keeps cookies on the app's first navigation from a notification/deep link; Strict can drop the session on cold deep-link opens) with a fixed cookie domain (.venuepro.asia) so TWA and web share state. Keep CSRF on /livewire/update and verify it works inside the WKWebView (Capacitor server.hostname/allowNavigation set to the real origin, not capacitor://, so cookies + CSRF + same-origin all hold). In the service worker: NEVER cache POST, /livewire/update, /login, OTP, payment, or any Set-Cookie response — restrict fetch handling to GET app-shell + static + clearly-timestamped stale-while-revalidate discovery (already in the spec). Use a CSP that permits the wrapper origin and blocks framing (frame-ancestors) so the shell can't be embedded maliciously. Do not put OTP/session tokens in localStorage; rely on HttpOnly cookies and, on native, Capacitor Secure Storage only for non-secret prefs.

requirements.requirement: Ship a service worker that gives a fast, safe offline app-shell and a controlled update story so a wrapped app never gets stuck on a stale shell or acts on stale availability — critical because a frozen SW in an installed app is a support nightmare.
requirements.how: Use a build-time precache (Workbox or a hashed-asset manifest from Vite) for the app-shell + CSS/JS/fonts/maskable icons; serve an offline fallback page; stale-while-revalidate ONLY read-only discovery/availability with a visible 'updated Xm ago' timestamp (bookings always re-validate server-side via the EXCLUDE constraint per the spec). Adopt skipWaiting + clients.claim behind an explicit in-app 'Update available — reload' toast (don't silently swap mid-session and break a Livewire request). Version the cache by build hash so a new deploy cleanly evicts old assets. Expose an app/SW version header so support can diagnose stuck installs.

requirements.requirement: Enforce a single source of truth where the web PWA is the product and native shells add only deltas behind feature detection, so you never fork the UI or maintain a parallel app codebase.
requirements.how: Centralise a tiny runtime 'platform' helper (isStandalone via display-mode media query, isTWA via document.referrer android-app://, isCapacitor via window.Capacitor) and gate native-only affordances (push prompt, biometric lock, native share, haptics, status-bar color) on it; everything else is identical Blade/Livewire. Keep native config (Bubblewrap twa-manifest, Capacitor capacitor.config.ts, AASA, assetlinks) checked into the same repo under a /native or /app directory so web + shells version together. CI builds the web bundle once; the TWA/Capacitor jobs consume that same origin. No business logic lives in the shells.

requirements.requirement: Add lightweight install/handoff UX now so web users convert to installed/app users and the later store apps inherit a warm audience — and so attribution distinguishes web vs TWA vs iOS.
requirements.how: Implement a custom 'Add to Home Screen' prompt for venue staff (capture beforeinstallprompt on Android; iOS shows a Safari share-sheet hint), shown contextually after a successful booking action rather than on load. Use the manifest start_url query param (source=twa / source=pwa) + a server-side 'app surface' header to segment analytics and, later, deep-link 'Open in app' banners. Keep this dashboard-only (staff retention); public booking flow stays frictionless SSR for SEO and conversion.

risks: Livewire auth in the wrapper: SameSite=Strict or a capacitor:// origin will silently drop the session/CSRF on cold deep-link or notification opens — must be SameSite=Lax with a real same-origin Capacitor config and tested inside WKWebView, or the app appears 'logged out' randomly.

Apple 4.2 minimum-functionality rejection: a Capacitor/PWABuilder shell that just loads the public site will be rejected; the iOS build must add real native value (push, biometric, share, offline) gated behind capability detection.

Digital Asset Links fingerprint mismatch: using the local upload-key SHA-256 instead of the Play App Signing fingerprint (or SW-caching assetlinks.json) breaks TWA verification, showing browser chrome and failing review — must serve a live, non-cached route with both fingerprints.

Service worker caching a POST/auth/payment or Set-Cookie response (or a frozen SW after deploy) corrupts state in an installed app and is very hard for users to clear — fetch handling must be GET-only with build-hash cache versioning and an explicit update toast.

iOS web push is install-gated and fragile (home-screen PWA only, user-gesture prompt, 16.4+); treating it as the primary iOS notification channel will under-deliver — native APNs via the Capacitor path is the real plan, web push is the fallback.

Play Store timeline: new personal developer accounts face the May-2026 closed-testing rule (12 testers / 14 continuous days) before production — 'fast release' still needs ~2+ weeks of testing lead time baked into planning.

lensOut.lens: Web performance engineer — Lighthouse >=99 (mobile) on EVERY route, not just homepage
lensOut.summary: On Lighthouse mobile, score is dominated by TBT (30%), LCP (25%), CLS (25%) — 80% combined — so the whole strategy is: keep main-thread JS near-zero on SSR public routes, make the LCP element a preloaded self-hosted AVIF with explicit dimensions, and reserve box for every dynamic element to hold CLS at 0. The routes at real risk are the discovery list (many images + map), the venue page (interactive map + reviews + booking widget), the booking calendar (slot grid + Livewire round-trips), and the dashboard (heaviest Livewire payloads); each needs a per-template asset budget and a per-route Lighthouse CI gate at minScore 0.99, not a single homepage check. Enforce it with hard budgets in CI (lhci assertMatrix + per-template byte budgets) so a regression on any one route fails the pipeline — "99 everywhere" is only real if every route is measured.

requirements.requirement: Lighthouse CI must run mobile audits against EVERY public route template AND a representative authenticated dashboard route, asserting categories:performance/accessibility/best-practices/seo each >= 0.99; any route below 0.99 fails the pipeline. Replace the spec's '>=98 on key pages' with '>=99 on every route class'.
requirements.how: Use @lhci/cli (0.15.x) with assertMatrix and matchingUrlPattern so each route class (/, /{sport}/{city}, /venue/{slug}, /venue/{slug}/book, /dashboard/*) gets categories:performance ['error',{minScore:0.99}]. Drive it from a GitHub Actions matrix (one URL per matrix leg, parallelised) against a seeded staging build with 3-8 anchor venues so the discovery list and map render real volume. Pin Lighthouse mobile preset (Moto-G-class CPU 4x throttle, 1.6Mbps RTT) and run numberOfRuns:3 taking median to kill flake. Seed an authed session via a cookie/token for the dashboard leg. Wire a separate budgets.json (resource-size + resource-count per type) as performance-budget assertions so byte regressions fail independently of the score.

requirements.requirement: Each route class must have an enforced byte/element budget. Targets (gzip/brotli, mobile): public SSR routes <= 30KB JS, <= 25KB critical CSS inlined + <= 20KB deferred CSS, LCP image <= 90KB AVIF; dashboard route <= 80KB JS (Livewire+Alpine core) + per-component HTML payload <= 40KB. TBT must stay < 150ms and CLS = 0 on every route.
requirements.how: Encode budgets in lighthouserc budgets.json (resourceSizes by type: script/stylesheet/image/font/total; resourceCounts to cap third-party=0). On the build side, set a Vite manualChunks split so Alpine/Livewire JS never loads on SSR public routes — public pages ship only a tiny progressive-enhancement bundle (map facade click handler, nav). Fail CI if any chunk for a public entrypoint imports the Livewire runtime. Add a bundle-size check (e.g. size-limit) in CI mapped to each Vite entrypoint so a stray import is caught before Lighthouse even runs.

requirements.area: Discovery list route (highest risk: image weight + map + list length)
requirements.requirement: The discovery/search results list (/{sport}/{city}, radius search) must hit 99 with a long venue list of image cards plus a map without blowing LCP/CLS/TBT. No eager map JS, no layout shift as cards/images load, image weight bounded.
requirements.how: Render the list fully SSR (no Livewire) with server-side pagination/cursor (no infinite-scroll JS on first paint). Every venue card image is a fixed-aspect-ratio with explicit width/height (or aspect-ratio CSS) + loading='lazy' + decoding='async' + fetchpriority='low' below fold; the first card image (if it is LCP) gets fetchpriority='high' and a matching the responsive AVIF srcset. Map is a STATIC self-hosted raster/vector thumbnail (facade) with a click-to-load interactive MapLibre — zero map JS on initial load. Use a single small sprite/inline SVG for rating stars/amenity icons (no icon-font, no per-icon request). Cap rendered cards per page (e.g. 20) so total image payload stays within budget; reserve skeleton boxes so async data never shifts layout (CLS=0).

requirements.area: Venue page route (risk: interactive map + reviews + booking entry)
requirements.requirement: The venue detail page (/venue/{slug}) must hit 99 despite a map, photo gallery, review list, and a booking CTA. The interactive map must never be on the LCP/TBT path, the gallery must not cause CLS, and schema.org/AggregateRating must be present for SEO=99.
requirements.how: Map facade: ship a self-hosted static map image as the visible element with a play-button overlay; lazily import MapLibre GL + self-hosted Jakarta PMTiles only on user click (dynamic import, so its JS/wasm is off the critical path and out of TBT). Gallery: AVIF/WebP <picture> with explicit dimensions and a CSS aspect-ratio container; first/hero image preloaded as LCP, rest lazy. Reviews: SSR-rendered (paginate, lazy 'load more' is a normal link, not JS). Booking CTA is a plain <a> to the booking route (no Livewire on this page). Embed SportsActivityLocation + AggregateRating + AggregateOffer JSON-LD server-side. Self-host all fonts (woff2, subset to Latin + needed Indonesian glyphs, font-display:swap, preload the single critical weight).

requirements.area: Booking calendar route (risk: Livewire round-trips + slot-grid interactivity + CLS)
requirements.requirement: The booking/slot-selection screen (/venue/{slug}/book) must hit 99 even though it is genuinely interactive (date picker, slot grid, hold timer). Keep Livewire payloads small, first paint SSR, and no layout shift when slots refresh.
requirements.how: Server-render the initial day's slot grid in the first HTML response (so LCP/FCP don't wait on a Livewire round-trip). Use Livewire 3 only for slot re-fetch on date change; keep the component HTML payload < 40KB by rendering only the slot grid partial (avoid re-sending the whole page — scope the component tightly). Use wire:navigate.hover prefetch for the venue->book transition so the page is warm before click. Drive instant slot UI feedback (selected state, disabled past slots) with Alpine locally to avoid a server round-trip per tap (lowers perceived latency and TBT spikes). Reserve fixed grid cell dimensions and a fixed-height hold-timer/error region so morph updates never shift layout (CLS=0). Debounce/guard rapid date taps so morphdom diffs stay cheap. The hold-timer countdown runs in Alpine (client clock), not via polling, to avoid repeated network/JS cost.

requirements.area: Dashboard PWA route (risk: heaviest Livewire payload + app-shell, must also be app-ready)
requirements.requirement: Authenticated Livewire dashboard routes (calendar, bookings, refunds, credit top-up) must each hit 99 mobile. These carry the largest JS (Livewire+Alpine) and biggest data tables, and must stay fast inside the future TWA/Capacitor wrapper.
requirements.how: Lazy-load heavy widgets: render the dashboard calendar via a server-paginated SSR-on-first-load partial, hydrate Livewire after. Virtualise/paginate large booking tables (don't morph 500 rows). Split Livewire components so a route only ships the JS/HTML it needs (no monolithic page component). Cache the app shell + hashed static assets in the service worker (stale-while-revalidate for read views, network-first for booking/credit mutations) so wrapper cold-starts feel instant. Keep CLS=0 with skeleton placeholders sized to the loaded content. Because the SW + manifest + same routes back the future Play Store (TWA/Bubblewrap) and iOS (Capacitor) wrappers, hold the dashboard to the same 99 bar so app-store performance is guaranteed by the web build.

requirements.area: TTFB / server response (LCP floor on all routes)
requirements.requirement: Server TTFB must be low and stable enough that LCP < 2.5s on throttled mobile across every route, including warm and cold. Slow TTFB caps LCP and silently drags every route's score below 99.
requirements.how: Run Laravel Octane + FrankenPHP worker mode (app stays booted in memory) for ~5-8x faster TTFB on cached pages vs php-fpm; set --max-requests to bound memory and audit singletons for cross-request state leaks (Octane is not drop-in). Cache hot discovery/availability read models in Redis (the spec already mandates Redis hot-availability cache) and serve SSR discovery/venue pages from a short-TTL full-response/fragment cache keyed by sport+city+filters. Set brotli + immutable long-cache headers on hashed assets, and HTTP/2+ so the preloaded LCP image and critical CSS arrive first. Add preconnect only to same-origin asset host (no external preconnect — zero-CDN rule).

requirements.requirement: All venue/court imagery must be served as responsive AVIF (WebP fallback) at multiple widths, with explicit intrinsic dimensions, self-hosted, and the LCP image preloaded — applied uniformly so no single image route (discovery card, venue hero, gallery) breaks the budget or causes CLS.
requirements.how: Build a server-side image pipeline (e.g. Intervention Image / Spatie Media Library + glide, or a queued transform job) that on upload generates AVIF+WebP at fixed widths (e.g. 320/640/960/1280) and stores intrinsic w/h. Templates emit with type='image/avif' + WebP fallback, srcset/sizes, and width/height attributes on the (kills CLS). Preload the LCP candidate per route with . Strip EXIF, cap quality (AVIF q~50-60 is visually fine) to keep hero <= 90KB / card <= 30KB. All under /storage or a local asset route — never an external CDN (keeps the gated-page zero-external-request rule and Best-Practices=99).

requirements.area: Critical CSS, fonts, and zero render-blocking
requirements.requirement: Every route must inline its critical (above-the-fold) CSS, defer the rest, and self-host a minimal subset font set so there is no render-blocking request and no FOIT/CLS from late fonts.
requirements.how: Generate per-template critical CSS (Tailwind output is small if purged hard; inline the above-the-fold slice in <head> and load the full hashed stylesheet with media='print' onload swap or rel=preload+rel=stylesheet). Subset the font (woff2, Latin + required Indonesian diacritics only), preload exactly one critical weight, font-display:swap, and set a metric-compatible fallback (size-adjust/ascent-override) so the swap causes no layout shift (CLS=0). Keep one font family / max two weights to stay within the font byte budget. Verify with Lighthouse 'eliminate render-blocking resources' and 'avoid layout shifts' audits = pass on every route in CI.

requirements.requirement: Structure the PWA so the future native wrappers (Play Store TWA/Bubblewrap, iOS Capacitor) ship fast with no Lighthouse regression: the same routes, manifest, and SW that score 99 on web are exactly what the wrapper loads.
requirements.how: Make the dashboard/admin a clean app-shell SPA-feel via Livewire wire:navigate (SPA transitions without re-downloading JS/CSS), a complete manifest (name, icons incl. maskable, theme/background color, display:standalone, start_url, scope), and a service worker precaching the shell. Maintain a Digital Asset Links (assetlinks.json) endpoint now so a TWA can be wrapped with zero code change later. Keep all routes same-origin and CSP/headers wrapper-safe. Because the wrapper just loads these routes, the existing per-route 99 gate already guarantees app-store performance — no separate native perf budget needed for v1.

risks: Map is the #1 score-killer: any eager Google Maps/MapLibre JS on discovery or venue pages will tank TBT (30% weight) and add external requests; the facade pattern (static image -> click-to-load) is mandatory, and CI must assert zero map JS on initial load.

Livewire dashboard payloads grow silently: a single page-level component morphing large tables can push HTML payload + TBT past budget on real data. Without seeded-volume staging audits and per-component payload caps, the dashboard quietly slips below 99.

CLS regressions are the sneakiest: any image without explicit dimensions, late web font without size-adjust, or async-loaded slot/review block without a reserved box silently costs CLS (25% weight). Needs a CI 'layout-shift-elements' assertion per route, not eyeballing.

Single-page testing illusion: optimizing only the homepage and assuming the rest follow is the core failure mode. The score is per-route; discovery-with-100-venues, a venue with 30 photos, and a busy booking grid behave nothing like the homepage — each must be in the CI matrix with realistic seeded data.

Octane/FrankenPHP introduces shared-process state bugs (leaked singletons, container state across requests) and package incompatibilities; if not audited it can cause wrong-tenant data or memory bloat that degrades TTFB/LCP under load. Mitigate with --max-requests, singleton audit, and load testing before relying on it for the LCP floor.

Self-hosted fonts/images raise origin bandwidth (no CDN offload): under traffic spikes, slow asset delivery hurts LCP across all routes. Mitigate with brotli, HTTP/2, aggressive immutable caching, and tight image budgets; revisit a self-hosted edge cache if origin LCP degrades.

Lighthouse lab !== field: hitting 99 in CI lab conditions doesn't guarantee good real-user CWV on low-end Jakarta Android over 3G/4G. Add field (CrUX/RUM) monitoring post-launch so the lab gate doesn't create false confidence.

lensOut.lens: Application Security Engineer (OWASP Top 10 + Laravel 12 hardening + payments/OTP/multi-tenancy + Indonesia UU PDP)
lensOut.summary: The spec's biggest concrete risks are OTP/WhatsApp abuse (SMS-pumping, enumeration, brute force), cross-tenant IDOR in the venue dashboard, webhook spoofing/replay that could fake "paid" status and trigger fraudulent venue refunds, and a CSP that must survive Livewire+Alpine inline JS while still blocking XSS. Because the platform stores Indonesian phone numbers and is non-custodial but still triggers venue-side money movement (refunds, credit deductions), UU PDP (Law 27/2022, 72h breach notice) and payment-flow fraud controls are mandatory, not optional. The controls below are version-accurate for Laravel 12 / Livewire 3 / Xendit xenPlatform and can be pasted into Section 7.4 as a security control checklist.

requirements.area: Auth / OTP abuse, brute force, enumeration
requirements.requirement: Phone/WhatsApp OTP must resist SMS-pumping (toll fraud), brute force, and account enumeration with layered, composite-key rate limits and constant-time, non-revealing responses.
requirements.how: Define Laravel 12 named limiters in a RateLimiter::for() block: 'otp-send' = Limit::perMinute(1)->by(phone) AND a daily cap (e.g. 5/day per phone) AND a per-IP cap (e.g. 10/hour) AND a per-IP-subnet/device cap; combine as multiple Limit objects returned from one limiter. Store OTPs hashed (Hash::make / HMAC), 6 digits, single-use, 5-min TTL, max 5 verify attempts then invalidate+force re-send (track attempts in Redis keyed by phone). Add exponential backoff + a cooldown after N failed sends. Normalise phone to E.164 (libphonenumber) before keying so '08...'/'+62...' can't bypass limits. Return identical generic responses and identical timing whether or not the phone exists (no 'user not found'); same for verify. Use a CAPTCHA/Turnstile challenge after threshold. Bind WhatsApp BSP spend alerts + a hard daily send budget circuit-breaker. Re-issue session ID on login (session fixation) and require OTP re-challenge for sensitive actions (refund execution, credit top-up, phone change).

requirements.requirement: Venue-staff and venue-owners must be provably unable to read or mutate any other venue's bookings, payments, credits, refunds, reviews, or settings; platform-admin elevation must not leak into tenant queries.
requirements.how: Bind the active venue from the route (route-model binding) NOT a global scope, then authorise every action with Laravel Policies (BookingPolicy, PaymentPolicy, CreditWalletPolicy, RefundPolicy) checking $user->venue_id === $model->venue_id. Use Gate::before only to grant platform_admin and ensure admin actions go through explicit admin-scoped queries, never withoutGlobalScopes on tenant code. Always resolve child records via the parent relationship ($venue->bookings()->findOrFail($id)) so a forged ID 404s instead of cross-loading. Never trust IDs from the request body for ownership. In Livewire, mark ownership/identity properties (#[Locked] $venueId, $bookingId) so the client cannot mutate them, and re-authorise inside every Livewire action method (Livewire public properties are untrusted input). Ship Pest isolation tests: staff of Venue A hitting Venue B's booking/payment/refund/credit/review endpoints and Livewire actions must all 403/404 — this is a required, blocking test suite per Section 9.

requirements.area: Payments — webhook signature, replay, status spoofing, refund fraud
requirements.requirement: The Xendit webhook is the only thing that flips a booking to paid/confirmed, deducts credits, and can trigger venue refunds — it must be unforgeable, idempotent, replay-proof, and resistant to amount/booking tampering.
requirements.how: Verify the x-callback-token header against the venue/platform's stored token using hash_equals() (constant-time), reject mismatch with 200-but-ignore or 401 logged. Insert webhook_events(event_id UNIQUE) FIRST in the same transaction; a duplicate insert (unique violation) means already-processed → ack and stop (idempotency). Enforce the allowed-transition matrix with a conditional UPDATE ... WHERE status='hold' AND hold_expires_at>now(); 0 rows = late/duplicate. Cross-check the webhook's amount and currency against the stored booking price before confirming — never trust the payload's amount. Treat the webhook payload as a notification only; where the SDK supports it, re-fetch the charge from Xendit by reference to confirm status (defence against forged payloads). Refund execution must be a separate, OTP/re-auth-gated, idempotent action keyed by booking with its own audit row, and must verify the booking is in a refundable state and that the refund amount matches the CancellationPolicy computation server-side (player cannot influence refund %). Rate-limit and alert on abnormal refund volume per venue (fraud signal). Webhook route is CSRF-exempt but token-protected; restrict by Xendit source IPs at the edge if available.

requirements.area: XSS / CSP with Livewire 3 + Alpine (OWASP A03)
requirements.requirement: A strict Content-Security-Policy must block injected scripts on both SSR public pages and the Livewire/Alpine dashboard without resorting to 'unsafe-eval' or 'unsafe-inline' on script-src.
requirements.how: Use the Livewire CSP-safe build so Alpine uses its CSP-safe evaluator (no eval); avoid complex inline Alpine expressions — move logic to Alpine.data()/component methods. Generate a per-request nonce in middleware and emit script-src 'nonce-{n}' 'strict-dynamic'; default-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'; frame-ancestors 'self'; form-action 'self'; pass it via @livewireScriptConfig(['nonce' => $nonce]) and to any first-party inline script. style-src 'self' (avoid 'unsafe-inline'; if Alpine x-bind:style or Tailwind requires it temporarily, isolate and plan removal). Because no external CDN is used (per spec), default-src 'self' is achievable and strong. Manage policy via spatie/laravel-csp policy classes with test coverage. Rely on Blade's default {{ }} auto-escaping; forbid {!! !!} on any user-derived content (venue descriptions, reviews, names) — sanitise rich text server-side with an allowlist (e.g. HTMLPurifier) if any HTML is permitted. Set a report-uri/report-to to catch violations in prod.

requirements.area: Mass assignment, validation, SQLi on raw PostGIS (OWASP A03/A08)
requirements.requirement: All write paths must be protected against mass-assignment of privileged fields, and the raw PostGIS/full-text radius queries must never concatenate user input.
requirements.how: Use explicit FormRequest validation + DTOs; never pass request()->all() into create/update. Guard sensitive columns (role, venue_id, status, balance, xendit_subaccount_id, price_idr, service_fee_idr) via $guarded or by never including them in $fillable, and set ownership/pricing server-side from authenticated context, not the request. For PostGIS radius search (ST_DWithin / geography) and full-text search, always use parameter bindings (DB::select(..., [$lng,$lat,$radius]) or query-builder bindings) — never string-interpolate lat/lng/radius/search terms; cast/validate them as numeric and clamp radius to a max. Validate sport/city/slug against allowlists. In Livewire, validate inside actions (rules()) — public properties are untrusted; never bind a model's mass-assignable attributes directly to wire:model without server-side re-validation. Enforce server-side authoritative pricing (recompute price + service fee from pricing_rules on the server at hold time; never trust client-sent amounts) to prevent price-tampering fraud.

requirements.area: Security headers, session, CSRF, file upload, SSRF
requirements.requirement: Baseline platform hardening: secure session/cookies, CSRF on all state-changing web routes, hardened headers, safe venue-image uploads, and SSRF protection on any server-side fetch.
requirements.how: Session: SESSION_SECURE_COOKIE=true, HttpOnly, SameSite=Lax (Strict where UX allows), regenerate on login/privilege change, short idle lifetime for dashboard/admin, encrypt session driver. CSRF: keep Laravel's VerifyCsrfToken on all web/Livewire routes; only the Xendit webhook is excluded (token-protected instead). Headers (middleware): Strict-Transport-Security (preload), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors, Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin, Permissions-Policy locking geolocation/camera/etc; force HTTPS + ->secure() URLs. File upload (venue/court photos): validate real MIME via finfo + extension allowlist (jpg/png/webp), max size, re-encode/strip EXIF (GPS/PII) via Intervention Image, store outside webroot on private disk with random names, serve via signed/temporary URLs, run through image processing to neutralise polyglots; never allow SVG (XSS) unless sanitised. SSRF: if the server ever fetches remote URLs (map tiles are self-hosted per spec, but webhook/BSP callbacks, image-by-URL imports), allowlist hosts, block private/link-local/metadata IP ranges, disable redirects to internal hosts. CORS: lock to first-party origins only.

requirements.area: PII / UU PDP (Law 27/2022) — phone numbers & data-subject rights
requirements.requirement: Phone numbers (and names/booking history) are personal data under Indonesia's UU PDP; the platform must have lawful basis, encryption, breach response within 72 hours, and data-subject rights — non-compliance risks fines up to 2% of annual revenue.
requirements.how: Record lawful basis per processing purpose (contract for booking; consent for marketing WA messages — separate, granular, withdrawable consent, logged with timestamp). Encrypt phone numbers at rest (Laravel encrypted casts or column-level encryption) and consider a blind index/HMAC for lookup so the raw number isn't queried in plaintext; mask phone numbers in dashboard UI and logs (show last 3-4 digits). Implement data-subject rights endpoints/flows: access (export), rectification, erasure, and consent withdrawal, with defined response SLAs; honour deletion while retaining minimal financial/audit records as legally required. Define a documented breach-notification runbook to notify the data subject and the Indonesian DPA within 72 hours (what data, when/how, mitigation). Add a privacy policy + data-processing notice; minimise retention (auto-purge stale OTPs, raw webhook payloads, abandoned holds). Treat WhatsApp BSP and Xendit as processors — have DPAs/contracts. Keep PII out of error tracking/log aggregation (scrub before send).

requirements.requirement: Security-relevant and money-relevant events must be captured in a tamper-evident, queryable audit trail that excludes raw secrets/PII but proves who did what.
requirements.how: Log (append-only / write to a separate store): OTP send+verify outcomes, logins/failures, role/permission changes, venue settings + cancellation-policy edits, credit top-ups/deductions/reversals, refund executions, webhook receipt + decision (accepted/duplicate/rejected) with event_id, and admin impersonation/elevation. Include actor id, tenant venue_id, IP, user-agent, timestamp, and before/after for sensitive fields (e.g. via spatie/laravel-activitylog or owen-it/laravel-auditing). Mask PII (phone) in audit entries. Alert on anomalies: refund spikes per venue, OTP send-rate breaches, repeated authorization (403) failures from one actor (IDOR probing), webhook signature failures. Ship logs to an external aggregator so a compromised app server can't erase them; protect against log injection by escaping user-controlled fields.

requirements.requirement: Xendit keys, webhook tokens, WA BSP keys, DB/Redis creds, and the app key must never leak, and third-party packages (incl. Xendit/Livewire) must be tracked for known CVEs.
requirements.how: Keep all secrets in env/secret manager, never in repo; rotate the Xendit webhook token + API keys on a schedule and on staff offboarding; ensure Redis requires a password (already noted in spec) and is not network-exposed. Use composer audit + npm audit / Dependabot in CI; pin and verify the Xendit SDK and spatie/laravel-permission versions (note: CVE-2026-47743 affected a Livewire-based admin package — monitor advisories for any admin Livewire deps). Run Enlightn or a Laravel security checker in CI. Lock APP_DEBUG=false in prod, disable Telescope/Horizon dashboards behind auth+IP allowlist, ensure stack traces never reach users. Verify webhook tokens differ per environment so a leaked staging token can't hit prod. Subresource-integrity is moot (no CDN) but verify the self-hosted asset build pipeline integrity.

requirements.requirement: Beyond the DB EXCLUDE constraint, the booking/hold/credit flow must resist inventory-griefing, hold-spamming, and credit-drain abuse.
requirements.how: Rate-limit hold creation per player/IP to stop an attacker mass-holding slots to deny inventory (holds expire in 10min but spamming still grieves); require a verified (OTP) session before creating a paid hold. Ensure expired-hold cleanup job is reliable (queue + retry) so abandoned holds free inventory and cancel the pending Xendit charge. Server recomputes price, service fee, and credit cost authoritatively at confirmation — client cannot reduce them. Make credit deduction strictly idempotent and tied to the same transaction as the booking confirmation (deduct exactly once per confirmed booking; reversal exactly once per qualifying cancellation) to prevent double-deduct/double-refund races. Guard the walk-in path with staff authorization + the same tenant policy so staff can't create bookings on another venue. Validate that 'venue-initiated full refund' (weather/maintenance) is staff-authorised, not player-triggerable.

risks: OTP/WhatsApp SMS-pumping (toll fraud): attackers trigger mass OTP sends to premium-rate numbers, draining the WA BSP budget — needs per-phone/per-IP/daily composite limits + spend circuit-breaker, not just one throttle.

Webhook spoofing or replay flipping bookings to 'paid' without real payment, or forged payloads with mismatched amounts — would confirm free bookings and could trigger erroneous venue refunds (money loss to venues). Constant-time token check + idempotency + amount cross-check + optional re-fetch from Xendit are all required.

Cross-tenant IDOR is the highest-likelihood data-breach vector given Livewire public properties are untrusted and the spec deliberately avoids global scopes; without per-action policies + #[Locked] + isolation tests, one venue can read another's PII/payments.

CSP regression: if Tailwind/Alpine forces 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval' back into script-src to make the dashboard work, the XSS protection collapses — must commit to the Livewire CSP-safe build early or the >=99 Lighthouse + security goals conflict late.

UU PDP non-compliance (phone numbers unencrypted, no 72h breach runbook, no consent separation for WA marketing) carries fines up to 2% of annual revenue and is now fully enforced (transition ended Oct 2024).
Price/refund-amount tampering via client-trusted values: if the server doesn't recompute price, service fee, and refund % authoritatively, players can manipulate booking cost or refund payout.

lensOut.summary: VenuePro needs one self-hosted design system serving two densities from a single token set: an airy, trustworthy public SSR site (conversion + SEO) and a compact, scannable Livewire dashboard (operator throughput). The recommendation is Plus Jakarta Sans (Indonesian-designed, excellent Latin + Bahasa diacritics, tabular numerals for IDR/time), self-hosted as subset woff2 with a system-font fallback metric-matched to avoid CLS, expressed entirely as Tailwind v4 @theme tokens (CSS-first, no JS config) on a 4/8pt spacing grid with a modular type scale, semantic color tokens with a sport-court accent, and tightly specified components (button/input/card/slot-grid/modal/nav). Everything is engineered to hold Lighthouse 99 mobile: total CSS budget under ~14KB critical, zero CDN, one preloaded variable-weight (or 2-axis subset) font, and shadow/radius tokens cheap enough to render with no layout thrash.

requirements.requirement: Adopt Plus Jakarta Sans as the single brand+UI typeface, self-hosted as subset woff2 with a metric-matched system fallback so there is zero CDN dependency and zero font-swap CLS (protects Lighthouse 99 and CLS=0).
requirements.how: Self-host via @fontsource/plus-jakarta-sans (or fontsource-variable) pinned in package.json and bundled by Vite — NOT Google Fonts/CDN. Ship ONE variable woff2 (weight axis 200-800) OR, if variable proves too large after subsetting, exactly two static cuts: 400 + 600 (700 only if a bolder display weight is wanted). Subset to latin + latin-ext (covers all Bahasa Indonesia diacritics; Indonesian uses no extra glyphs beyond latin) with glyphhanger/pyftsubset, keeping the tabular-figure and required punctuation glyphs. Declare @font-face with font-display:swap and unicode-range; <link rel="preload" as="font" type="font/woff2" crossorigin> ONLY the single critical weight used above the fold. Provide a fallback stack with size-adjust/ascent-override/descent-override (use Fontaine or capsize-generated metrics for Arial/Roboto) so the fallback occupies identical box metrics — eliminates reflow on swap. Token: --font-sans: "Plus Jakarta Sans", var(--font-fallback). Budget the font(s) under ~45KB total transferred.

requirements.requirement: Define a fixed modular type scale as Tailwind v4 @theme --text-* tokens with line-height, letter-spacing and default weight baked into each step, tuned so dashboard data reads at 14px and public marketing reads larger, with a strict 16px minimum on form inputs (prevents iOS zoom-on-focus, an 'easy to use' + Lighthouse tap concern).
requirements.how: In app.css @theme: --text-xs:0.75rem/1rem (12/16, labels, tabular meta); --text-sm:0.875rem/1.25rem (14/20, dashboard body + table cells — the dashboard default); --text-base:1rem/1.5rem (16/24, public body + ALL inputs); --text-lg:1.125rem/1.625rem; --text-xl:1.25rem/1.75rem; --text-2xl:1.5rem/2rem; --text-3xl:1.875rem/2.25rem; --text-4xl:2.25rem/2.5rem; --text-5xl:3rem/1.05 (public hero only). Attach per-step --text-*--line-height and tighten --text-*--letter-spacing to -0.01em on >=2xl display sizes, 0 on body. Weights: --font-weight-normal:400 (body), -medium:500 (UI labels/buttons), -semibold:600 (headings, prices, court names), -bold:700 (hero only). Numerals: apply font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums via a .nums utility (or @layer base on .price/.time/td-numeric) so IDR amounts and 18:00-19:30 slot times align in columns. Format IDR as 'Rp 250.000' (id-ID dot grouping) — bake into a Blade @rupiah helper / Number::currency, not the font.

requirements.requirement: Standardize on a 4px base / 8px rhythm spacing scale shared by both surfaces, with two documented density modes (airy public vs compact dashboard) achieved by swapping component padding tokens — not by inventing ad-hoc margins.
requirements.how: Tailwind v4 already exposes --spacing:0.25rem (4px) generating the full n*4px scale; keep it and use only steps 1,2,3,4,6,8,10,12,16,20,24. Define semantic padding tokens layered on top: public card pad = p-6/p-8 (24/32), section vertical rhythm = py-16/py-24 mobile-up; dashboard card pad = p-3/p-4 (12/16), table cell = px-3 py-2, control gap = gap-2/gap-3. Touch targets: every interactive element min 44x44px (min-h-11) regardless of visual size — wrap small icon buttons in a 44px hit area. Container: --container token max-w 1200px public, full-bleed fluid dashboard with a 16px gutter (px-4) on mobile rising to px-6/px-8. Document the rule: 'spacing only from the scale; density is a token swap, never a one-off pixel value.'

requirements.requirement: Ship a semantic color token layer (not raw palette names in markup) in OKLCH for perceptual consistency, with an accent evoking court/sport, AA-contrast-guaranteed text tokens, and explicit status colors for booking states (hold/paid/confirmed/cancelled/no_show).
requirements.how: @theme define a brand ramp and map to SEMANTIC tokens components actually use: --color-bg, --color-surface, --color-surface-muted, --color-border, --color-text, --color-text-muted, --color-primary (+ -hover/-fg), --color-accent, plus status tokens --color-success/warning/danger/info each with a -subtle (bg tint) and -fg pair. Pick a deep court-green or padel-blue primary (e.g. primary ~oklch(0.55 0.13 200) teal-blue) with white -fg; verify every text-on-bg pair >=4.5:1 (large >=3:1) — this is a Lighthouse a11y gate. Map booking statuses to badge tokens: hold=warning-subtle, paid=info-subtle, confirmed=success-subtle, cancelled=neutral/danger-subtle, no_show=danger-subtle. Use OKLCH literals (Tailwind v4 native) for smooth state ramps. Never write bg-green-500 in templates — only bg-success/bg-primary semantic utilities so re-theming is one-file.

requirements.requirement: Define a small radius set and a deliberately shallow, cheap elevation scale (Lighthouse-safe shadows) so the UI feels soft/comfortable without heavy multi-layer shadows that hurt paint performance or read as 'AI default'.
requirements.how: @theme: --radius-sm:0.375rem(6), --radius-md:0.5rem(8, default control/input), --radius-lg:0.75rem(12, cards), --radius-xl:1rem(16, modals/sheets), --radius-2xl:1.25rem, --radius-full:9999px (pills/avatars/slot chips). Elevation as tokens: --shadow-xs (1px subtle border-substitute), --shadow-sm (cards at rest), --shadow-md (raised/hover), --shadow-lg (dropdown/popover), --shadow-xl (modal/bottom-sheet). Keep each to ONE or at most two layers with low alpha (rgb 0 0 0 /0.04-0.10) — prefer a 1px border + shadow-xs over a big blur for resting cards. No shadow on the slot grid (use borders) to keep the data-dense calendar fast and crisp.

requirements.area: Core components (button / input / card / nav / modal)
requirements.requirement: Specify the primitive components against the tokens with explicit states (default/hover/active/focus-visible/disabled/loading) and an accessible focus ring, delivered as Blade components for SSR public and matching Livewire/Blade components for the dashboard so the two surfaces are visually identical.
requirements.how: Button: variants primary/secondary/ghost/danger; sizes sm(h-9 px-3 text-sm)/md(h-11 px-4 text-sm/base)/lg(h-12 px-6); radius-md; weight-medium; visible :focus-visible ring (2px ring + 2px offset in --color-primary); :disabled opacity-50 cursor-not-allowed; loading shows inline spinner + keeps width (no CLS). Input/select/textarea: h-11, px-3, text-base(16px min), radius-md, 1px --color-border, focus = primary ring; error state = danger border + helper text slot + aria-describedby; label always present (a11y + ease of use). Card: surface bg, radius-lg, border + shadow-sm, header/body/footer slots, two density paddings. Nav: public = sticky translucent top bar (backdrop-blur, height 56-64px) collapsing to hamburger; dashboard = persistent left rail >=lg collapsing to a 56px bottom tab bar on mobile (PWA app feel). Modal: centered dialog desktop, bottom-sheet on mobile (radius-xl top corners), trap focus, Esc + scrim close, role=dialog/aria-modal. Build all as <x-ui.button> etc. so SSR and Livewire share one source.

requirements.requirement: Give the time-slot booking grid and dashboard calendar a dedicated, token-driven spec — it is the most-used and most data-dense surface — optimized for thumb tapping on mobile and dense scanning on desktop, with unambiguous slot states.
requirements.how: Slot chip: pill/rounded-md, min 44px tall, tabular-nums time label (18:00), state via semantic tokens — available (surface + border, primary text), selected (primary bg + primary-fg), unavailable/blocked (surface-muted + muted text + diagonal hatch or lock icon, aria-disabled), peak vs off-peak distinguished by a price line + a subtle accent left-border (NOT color alone — colorblind-safe, add a 'Peak' text/icon). Layout: mobile = vertical scroll list of time rows grouped by morning/afternoon/evening with sticky group headers; desktop dashboard = CSS-grid columns=courts, rows=time bands, 1px grid lines (no shadows). Show price + duration on each slot. Sold/held slots keep their box (no reflow). Dashboard day-view: compact --text-sm, color-coded booking blocks using status tokens, current-time indicator line. Re-validate availability server-side on tap regardless of cached paint (matches spec's stale-while-revalidate note).

requirements.area: Density modes (data dashboard vs airy public)
requirements.requirement: Encode the two-personality requirement (comfortable/airy marketing vs efficient/compact operator tool) as a switchable density context rather than two separate stylesheets, so they share tokens, font payload, and component code — keeping CSS small for Lighthouse.
requirements.how: Set a root context: <body data-density="comfortable"> on public SSR, data-density="compact" on the dashboard/admin shell. In CSS map density to a handful of vars (--pad-card, --row-h, --control-h, --text-body, --gap) and have components consume those vars; compact overrides them via [data-density=compact]{...}. This avoids duplicating component classes and keeps one purged stylesheet. Public defaults: text-base body, generous py-16/24 sections, card p-6/8. Compact defaults: text-sm body, row-h 36-40px, control-h 36px, card p-3/4, tables with zebra/hover rows. Same buttons/inputs/cards — only the density vars change.

requirements.requirement: Design tokens dark-mode-ready from day one (especially for the dashboard, used in dim venue back-offices and as a future app), but ship light-first in v1 and gate dark behind a class toggle so it never adds render cost or risk to the Lighthouse-critical public pages.
requirements.how: Author all semantic tokens (bg/surface/text/border/status) as light values under :root and provide a .dark { } override block flipping them; because components reference only semantic tokens, dark mode is a token-file change with no markup churn. Use Tailwind v4 custom-variant @custom-variant dark (&:where(.dark, .dark *)) and toggle a .dark class on <html> via a tiny inline pre-paint script (read localStorage/prefers-color-scheme before first paint to avoid a flash). Keep dark mode OFF/optional on public SSR in v1 to protect the perf budget; enable first in the dashboard PWA. Ensure dark variants also pass AA contrast.

requirements.requirement: Treat the design system itself as a Lighthouse-99 constraint: a single bundled, purged, hashed CSS file with a hard byte budget and critical CSS inlined, with a documented rule that all visual values come from @theme tokens (no arbitrary values in templates) so the build stays small and consistent.
requirements.how: One app.css importing tailwindcss + the @theme token block + minimal @layer components for the primitives above; Vite builds it minified, brotli-compressed, content-hashed, long-cached. Inline critical CSS for the public hero/nav (above-the-fold) and async-load the rest, or accept a single small CSS file if total stays under ~14KB gzip critical. Enforce in CI: a stylesheet-size check + 'no arbitrary Tailwind values (bracket syntax) in Blade' lint so density/spacing/color always resolve to tokens. Document the full token table (this list) in the spec as the single source of truth; any new component must compose existing tokens. This keeps every page — not just the homepage — at Lighthouse 99 and gives the future TWA/Capacitor app a stable token contract.

risks: Variable-weight Plus Jakarta Sans woff2 may be too heavy even subset; if it pushes the font payload past ~45KB or regresses LCP, fall back to two static cuts (400/600) — measure both with Lighthouse on a low-end Android profile before committing.

Without size-adjust/ascent-override metric matching on the system fallback, the swap from fallback to Plus Jakarta Sans will cause CLS and can knock Lighthouse below 99 — the Fontaine/capsize fallback metrics are mandatory, not optional.

iOS Safari zooms on focus for inputs under 16px; the dashboard's compact 14px body must NOT apply to form inputs (inputs stay text-base/16px) or usability and Lighthouse tap-target/best-practices suffer.

Encoding peak/off-peak and booking status with color alone fails colorblind users and Lighthouse a11y contrast checks — every status must carry a text label/icon/pattern in addition to its token color.

Two density modes risk drifting into two de-facto design systems if teams add one-off paddings; the 'no arbitrary values, density is a token swap' CI lint is what keeps the CSS small and Lighthouse-safe — if not enforced, the budget erodes page by page.
Inlining critical CSS plus a service-worker-cached shell can serve stale tokens after a redesign; cache-bust via content-hashed filenames and a SW versioning strategy so token updates actually ship.

Tailwind v4 OKLCH colors and @custom-variant dark are version-specific; pin Tailwind v4 and verify the @theme token output in the actual build, since v3 tailwind.config.js syntax will silently not apply.

crossOut.lens: SEO (technical + local SEO for Jakarta; now also AI/answer-engine optimization)

tensions.withLens: PWA + mobile-app packaging engineer
tensions.tension: The PWA lens wants the dashboard/admin as a 'persistent app-shell SPA driven by wire:navigate' and ships a service worker. Two SEO-fatal risks if this leaks onto the PUBLIC surface: (1) an app-shell/SPA model serves an empty HTML skeleton hydrated by JS — in 2026 Googlebot still does delayed two-wave JS rendering AND AI crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot) do NOT execute JavaScript at all, so any public route that depends on wire:navigate/Livewire to inject venue names, prices, hours, or schema is invisible to answer engines and partially invisible to Google. (2) A service worker that caches the app-shell can serve a generic shell to a crawler or strip the per-page <head> (canonical, title, meta description, JSON-LD), and SW navigation-fallback rewrites can mask 404/410 status codes — Google needs true HTTP status codes for indexing hygiene.
tensions.reconciliation: Hard-partition the two surfaces at the routing/SW layer, which the PWA lens already half-concedes ('public SSR pages stay multi-page'). Make it a non-negotiable rule in the spec: (a) Public discovery/venue/booking routes are fully server-rendered HTML on FIRST byte — every indexable fact (venue name, address/NAP, hours, price, AggregateRating, JSON-LD) present in the raw HTML before any JS; wire:navigate may be used only as a progressive-enhancement page-transition over already-complete documents, never as the content source, and each destination must still return a complete standalone SSR document with its own <head>. (b) The service worker scope is restricted to the authenticated /dashboard and /admin paths (or uses a NetworkFirst-with-no-HTML-fallback strategy on public routes); the SW must NEVER cache or rewrite public HTML responses, never serve an app-shell fallback for a public navigation, and must pass through real 200/301/404/410 status codes. (c) Add a CI test (the spec already has a 'no external request' check) that fetches every public route with JS disabled and asserts title, canonical, meta description, h1, and JSON-LD are present — same harness the perf lens uses. This gives the PWA lens its installable app-shell for the operator app while guaranteeing the public surface stays crawler/AI-visible.

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer
tensions.tension: Mostly ALIGNED — both of us want zero/near-zero JS on public routes and SSR-first, and the perf lens's per-route Lighthouse-CI matrix already asserts category seo>=0.99, which protects me. Two friction points: (1) Lighthouse's 'seo' category is shallow — it checks meta/robots/crawlable links/legible font but does NOT validate JSON-LD correctness, hreflang, canonical consistency, or NAP — a route can score seo:1.0 and still be schema-broken or duplicate-content. (2) The perf lens wants the interactive map 'never on the LCP/TBT path' via a click-to-load facade; I need the venue's address/geo/NAP and map context to be crawlable, and a pure JS-facade map renders no indexable location text.
tensions.reconciliation:
(1) Add a dedicated SEO-validation gate ALONGSIDE Lighthouse in the same CI matrix: run a structured-data validator (schema.org/Google Rich Results test rules) + a canonical/hreflang/meta linter on every public route, asserting valid SportsActivityLocation/Offer/AggregateRating + exactly one self-canonical + matching og/twitter — so seo>=0.99 is necessary but not sufficient. (2) The static-map facade satisfies BOTH lenses precisely because the indexable location signal is NOT the map tiles — it is the SSR text: full postal address in
with hCard/JSON-LD PostalAddress + geo coordinates in JSON-LD + a noscript/SSR static of the map with descriptive alt. Crawlers read the structured address; users click to load interactive tiles. So keep the facade, but require the address/geo to be real SSR DOM text and JSON-LD, not baked only into the map image.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer
tensions.tension: The strict CSP (no unsafe-inline on script-src, nonce/hash-based) is SEO-friendly overall, but two collisions: (1) If JSON-LD is injected via <script type="application/ld+json"> the CSP must still allow it — script-src applies to type=ld+json blocks in some configurations; a too-aggressive nonce-only policy can suppress structured data and break Rich Results. (2) Security wants hardened headers and possibly aggressive bot/rate-limiting + WAF on public routes; over-broad bot mitigation or a JS/cookie challenge on public HTML can block or cloak content to Googlebot/Bingbot/GPTBot/ClaudeBot, and serving different HTML to bots vs users is 'cloaking' which Google penalizes.
tensions.reconciliation: (1) JSON-LD blocks are data, not executable script — emit them server-side and either give them the page nonce or (cleaner) keep them as static SSR output and verify with the Rich Results test in CI that the policy doesn't strip them; explicitly document that ld+json must render under the production CSP. (2) Co-author the bot policy: maintain an allowlist of verified search/AI crawler user-agents (reverse-DNS-verified Googlebot/Bingbot, plus GPTBot/ClaudeBot/PerplexityBot governed by robots.txt only) that bypass JS-challenge/interstitial rate limits on PUBLIC GET routes; never serve crawler-specific HTML (no cloaking) — the same SSR document goes to everyone, security challenges apply only to state-changing POST/auth/OTP routes. Rate-limit by composite key but exempt verified crawlers on read-only discovery endpoints.

tensions.withLens: UI / visual design-system architect
tensions.tension: Strongly ALIGNED (self-hosted subset woff2 with metric-matched fallback directly protects my Core Web Vitals/CLS which is a ranking signal, and zero-CDN helps). Minor frictions: (1) Plus Jakarta Sans subset must include the FULL Indonesian/Bahasa diacritic + IDR/× character set used in indexable on-page text — an over-aggressive Latin-basic subset that drops characters appearing in venue names/addresses (é, ·, Rp ligature, en-dash in time ranges) would render tofu in content Google reads, or force a fallback that hurts CLS. (2) The 14px compact dashboard density is fine (dashboard is noindex), but any compact/small-font treatment must NOT bleed onto public pages where legible-font-size is a Lighthouse SEO check and readability affects engagement/dwell.
tensions.reconciliation: (1) Define the font subset by the actual content character set (Bahasa Indonesia letters + punctuation used in addresses/prices/time-ranges), not a generic 'Latin' subset, and unicode-range-split so the diacritic block still loads; validate in CI that no indexable template renders a fallback-only glyph. (2) Bind font-size to the density context the design lens already proposes: public 'airy' density uses >=16px body (also satisfies the design lens's 16px form-input rule and prevents iOS zoom), compact 14px is scoped to the noindex dashboard only — encode this in tokens so small fonts can never leak to indexable routes.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer (refund disclosure) + product 'conversion' goal
tensions.tension: The spec mandates 'hard, prominent, unmissable refund/cancellation disclosures on every transaction page before payment' — necessary for trust/legal and indirectly for review quality (which is ~16-20% of local ranking). Conversion-optimization instinct (and an aggressive perf budget) might push to defer/collapse this disclosure or load it via JS. For SEO: deferring it to JS hides it from crawlers, and the transaction page itself is typically noindex — so the disclosure has near-zero direct ranking cost but high trust value.
tensions.reconciliation: No real conflict once framed correctly: render the disclosure as SSR static text/details element (zero JS, zero perf cost, satisfies the perf budget) on the (noindex) booking/transaction pages. Surface a SHORT, indexable summary of the venue's cancellation policy on the PUBLIC venue page as crawlable text — this is positive SEO (unique content + trust signals that improve review/return rates), and it pre-sets player expectations which reduces disputes and protects the venue's AggregateRating, my #2 local-ranking lever after proximity.

finalRequirements: [must] SSR-first, JS-independent public surface (now an AI-crawler requirement, not just Googlebot): Every PUBLIC route (/, /{sport}/{city}, /{sport}/{city}/{venue-slug}, /venue/{slug}, /venue/{slug}/book) must serve ALL indexable content — venue name, NAP/postal address, geo, operating hours, price/AggregateOffer, AggregateRating, breadcrumbs, and all JSON-LD — in the raw server-rendered HTML on first byte, with zero dependency on JavaScript/Livewire/wire:navigate to populate content. Rationale: in 2026 Googlebot still does delayed two-wave JS rendering and AI/answer crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot) do NOT execute JS at all; JS-injected content is invisible to them. wire:navigate is allowed ONLY as a transition over documents that are each independently complete SSR HTML.

[must] Service worker must not sabotage indexing: SW scope is restricted to authenticated /dashboard + /admin. On public routes the SW must never serve an app-shell fallback, never cache/rewrite public HTML, and must pass through true HTTP status codes (200/301/308/404/410). Add a CI assertion that public routes fetched with JS disabled contain a valid <title>, single self-referential <link rel=canonical>, meta description, exactly one <h1>, and parseable JSON-LD.

[must] Structured-data + on-page SEO validation as a CI gate, SEPARATE from Lighthouse: Lighthouse seo>=0.99 is necessary but insufficient (it ignores JSON-LD correctness, canonical/hreflang consistency, and NAP). Add a per-route structured-data validator + meta linter to the same CI matrix asserting: valid SportsActivityLocation (with PostalAddress, geo, openingHoursSpecification, telephone, priceRange), AggregateOffer/Offer (IDR), AggregateRating from venue_reviews, BreadcrumbList, and (homepage/org) Organization+WebSite with SearchAction; exactly one self-canonical; og:/twitter: present and consistent; <html lang="id">.

[must] Clean URL + crawl architecture and sitemap: Lock the IA: /{sport}/{city} (Jakarta district pages, e.g. /padel/jakarta-selatan) as primary local-landing pages, /venue/{slug} for venues, stable booking deep-links that match the PWA lens's URL scheme. Generate a chunked, lastmod-accurate sitemap index (venues, district landing pages, sport pages) updated on venue create/update; robots.txt that allows verified search+AI crawlers on public GET, disallows /dashboard, /admin, /book confirmation, and all query-param/filter permutations (faceted-search crawl traps) via canonical-to-base + robots rules. Discovery filters must use crawlable <a href> links (not JS-only) for the canonical facets we WANT indexed, and rel=canonical to the base list for the ones we don't.

[must] Local SEO for Jakarta is two-sided: website + Google Business Profile as one entity. In 2026 GBP signals (~32%) + proximity (~55%) + reviews (~16-20%) dominate local pack ranking and a strong site cannot be replaced by GBP nor vice-versa. Therefore: (a) consistent NAP rendered as SSR <address> + PostalAddress JSON-LD on every venue page, matching exactly what the venue uses on GBP; (b) build a venue-onboarding step + admin tooling that captures/links each venue's Google Business Profile and pushes consistent NAP/hours/category, and surfaces a 'claim/verify your GBP' checklist — VenuePro must act as a NAP-consistency engine across site + GBP + IG/WA; (c) per-district landing pages with genuinely unique localized copy (Bahasa) to win 'padel jakarta selatan'-type queries, gated by the spec's liquidity threshold so we never publish thin/empty district pages.

[must] Reviews are a ranking asset, protect and surface them: venue_reviews drive AggregateRating (JSON-LD on the public venue page, a direct local signal and rich-result eligibility) AND feed GBP review velocity. Render review count/score as crawlable SSR text + Review/AggregateRating schema; add a post-play WA/email prompt to leave a review (reuses the notification pipeline) to grow review velocity, which is a top-3 local factor. Show a short, crawlable summary of the venue's cancellation policy on the public venue page (SSR text) to pre-set expectations and protect rating quality.

[must] Internationalization done correctly for id-first, EN-later: ship <html lang="id"> and id locale in v1; design URLs and templates so that when EN is added it is via path-prefix (/en/...) with reciprocal hreflang (id <-> en) and x-default, and self-canonical per locale — never auto-translate or geo-redirect (which breaks crawling). Keep currency/time as IDR/WIB in both schema and visible text.

[should] No cloaking; crawler-aware security policy (co-owned with Security): the SAME SSR HTML is served to users and bots. Maintain a reverse-DNS-verified allowlist (Googlebot, Bingbot) and robots-governed AI crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot) that are exempt from JS-challenge/interstitial/aggressive rate limiting on public GET routes; security challenges apply only to POST/auth/OTP/state-changing routes. Ensure the production CSP does not strip <script type="application/ld+json"> (verify via Rich Results in CI).

[should] Core Web Vitals are a ranking signal, so the perf lens's >=99 matrix IS an SEO requirement — endorse it: keep CLS=0 (reserve box for images/maps/reviews), LCP<2.5s via preloaded self-hosted AVIF, font subset by actual Bahasa/IDR content character set with metric-matched fallback (no tofu in indexable text), and >=16px body on public 'airy' density (small 14px confined to noindex dashboard). These overlap the perf and design lenses; flag any conflict only if a perf cut would remove indexable text/JSON-LD from initial HTML.

[should] Answer-Engine/AEO readiness (the 2026 delta others missed): since AI crawlers can't run JS and increasingly drive discovery, ensure clean SSR semantics for extraction — concise factual SSR copy blocks (what/where/price/hours/sports), FAQPage JSON-LD on venue pages (court types, pricing bands, cancellation, amenities), entity-consistent naming, and allow GPTBot/ClaudeBot/PerplexityBot in robots.txt so VenuePro venues can surface in AI answers for 'where to play padel in Jakarta' queries.
missedByOthers: AI/answer-engine crawlers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot) do NOT execute JavaScript in 2026 — every other lens framed SSR-vs-SPA purely as a Googlebot/perf tradeoff. This RAISES the stakes on the public-surface SSR boundary: the PWA app-shell model, if it leaks onto public routes, makes the site invisible to the fastest-growing discovery channel, and there is no 'two-wave eventually renders it' safety net for AI crawlers. The spec needs an explicit AEO/GEO requirement and robots.txt that admits these crawlers.

Local SEO is TWO-SIDED and the spec only addresses the on-site half. With GBP at ~32% and proximity at ~55% of local-pack ranking, VenuePro's biggest ranking lever for its venues is making each venue's Google Business Profile + NAP + reviews consistent with the site — Google scores the business as one entity. No lens mentioned GBP integration, NAP-consistency tooling, or per-district landing pages; these belong in venue onboarding + admin, not just the perf/markup layer.

Faceted/filtered discovery (sport x district x price x availability) is a classic crawl-trap and duplicate-content risk. The perf lens worried about the discovery list's image/map weight but nobody addressed the SEO hazard of infinite filter-permutation URLs: needs canonical-to-base, robots rules, and a deliberate decision about WHICH facets (e.g. /padel/jakarta-selatan) are indexable landing pages vs noindex parameter noise.

hreflang/i18n future-proofing: the spec says 'Locale id (EN later)' as a one-liner, but adding EN later WITHOUT a pre-planned path-prefix + reciprocal hreflang + per-locale canonical architecture causes duplicate-content and wrong-locale indexing. This must be designed into the URL/template layer now even though only id ships in v1.

Lighthouse's 'seo' category is shallow and will give false confidence — it does not validate JSON-LD correctness, canonical/hreflang, or NAP consistency. The perf lens's seo>=0.99 gate can pass on a page with broken schema or duplicate canonicals. A separate structured-data + meta validation gate is required.

The cancellation-policy disclosure (mandated by spec/security) has a positive SEO use the others didn't see: a crawlable policy summary + FAQPage on the public venue page is unique content + a trust signal that improves review quality and reduces disputes — turning a compliance burden into an SEO/AEO asset at zero perf cost (SSR static text).

crossOut.lens: PWA + mobile-app packaging engineer ("what must the PWA do NOW to ship as a fast mobile app from one codebase, Play Store TWA + iOS")

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer (Lighthouse >=99 every route) + PWA/offline service worker
tensions.tension: The perf lens wants a service worker for offline app-shell + stale-while-revalidate, and the spec's 7.3 already prescribes SWR for discovery/availability. But a service worker that caches GET-aggressively will (a) silently cache Livewire's authenticated app-shell HTML and (b) collide with Livewire 3's OWN sessionStorage-based wire:navigate back-button cache and its POST /livewire/update round-trips. In an INSTALLED/wrapped app a frozen SW is unrecoverable by the user (no 'clear site data' button), so the exact SWR strategy that helps Lighthouse can brick the future TWA on a stale shell or leak one tenant's cached dashboard to the next login.
tensions.reconciliation: Split the SW by surface and method, and make it self-healing. (1) NEVER cache anything under /livewire/update, /dashboard/*, /admin/*, or any non-GET request, and never cache responses with Set-Cookie or Cache-Control: private — bypass straight to network. (2) Apply precache + SWR ONLY to public SSR routes and versioned static assets (the hashed Vite bundles, fonts, the static-map facade image). (3) Use a hashed-asset cache name keyed to the build id and an activate handler that deletes old caches, plus skipWaiting + clients.claim gated behind an in-app 'update available' prompt so a wrapped app can force-refresh. (4) Let Livewire own intra-shell navigation (wire:navigate) and let the SW own only the offline fallback page + static shell. This keeps Lighthouse PWA/perf credit AND guarantees no auth/tenant leakage and no frozen wrapped shell.

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer (app-shell vs SSR multi-page) + SEO/local-SEO lens
tensions.tension: My lens wants the dashboard structured as a persistent app-shell SPA (static top bar + bottom tab nav, wire:navigate, no full reloads) so it feels native when wrapped. The SEO/perf lens wants public discovery/venue/booking pages to stay independent SSR multi-page documents (each its own indexable URL, each independently scoring 99). If anyone tries to unify these into ONE shell, either the public pages lose their per-URL SSR indexability (SEO dies) or the dashboard gets full reloads (feels non-native when wrapped).
tensions.reconciliation: Two explicitly separate routing surfaces sharing only design tokens, never one shell. Surface A = public SSR multi-page (no app-shell, no bottom nav, each route a clean indexable document — this is what TWA/iOS deep-links INTO but is not the installed 'app home'). Surface B = authenticated Livewire app-shell SPA behind /dashboard and /admin (persistent chrome, wire:navigate, bottom tab nav) — this is the installed-app home and the manifest start_url. The manifest start_url points at the dashboard/login, NOT the marketing homepage, so the installed app opens to the operator tool while public SEO pages stay standalone. No conflict: they are different products at different URL prefixes.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer (strict CSP, session/CSRF, OTP, tenancy)
tensions.tension: Two collisions. (1) The security lens mandates a strict CSP with no unsafe-inline/unsafe-eval on script-src. A WebView wrapper (Capacitor) and Chrome Custom Tabs (TWA) inject their own bridge/runtime; a too-strict connect-src/frame-ancestors or a CSP that forbids the capacitor:// or https://localhost origin will break the bridge or block the bundled app from calling the API. (2) The security lens hardens session cookies (SameSite, Secure, HttpOnly) and CSRF on all state-changing routes — but SameSite=Strict cookies and CSRF tokens are exactly what silently breaks Livewire auth inside Custom Tabs / WKWebView, the single most common way a wrapped Livewire app dies.
tensions.reconciliation: (1) Keep script-src strict (nonce-based, no unsafe-inline) but extend connect-src and form-action to include the app's bundled origin (capacitor://localhost on iOS, https://<twa-host> on Android) and set frame-ancestors appropriately; ship a CSP variant header for app-origin requests rather than loosening the public CSP. (2) Use SameSite=Lax (not Strict) for the session cookie so OAuth/OTP redirects and Custom-Tab handoffs keep the session, keep Secure+HttpOnly, and for the iOS bundled-app path call a token/Sanctum API (Bearer) for state-changing actions instead of cookie+CSRF — so CSRF protection stays intact on web while the wrapped app uses a token the WebView can carry. Test the auth round-trip INSIDE both containers as an acceptance gate, not just in desktop Chrome.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer (XSS/PII) + my own original 'thin shell over same origin' claim
tensions.tension: My original recommendation — Capacitor pointing server.url at the live origin so the WebView just loads venuepro.asia — is BOTH a security and an Apple-compliance liability, and my own research confirms server.url is not production-recommended and is the classic Guideline 4.2 'thin web wrapper' rejection. Loading the entire remote origin in a WKWebView also widens the XSS blast radius into native bridge APIs and makes the CSP the only thing standing between an injected script and native capabilities.
tensions.reconciliation: Correct my own lens: do NOT ship the iOS app as a remote-origin WebView. Bundle a thin local web build (app-shell HTML/CSS/JS) inside the Capacitor app, talk to Laravel via a versioned JSON/Sanctum API for data, use Capacitor OTA (Cap-go/CapacitorUpdater) for fast web-layer updates without re-review, and add genuine native value (push, biometric unlock, offline booking queue, share-sheet, secure storage) so it clears Guideline 4.2 minimum-functionality. Android TWA may still point at the live origin (TWA is a real Chrome, not a thin wrapper, and is accepted) — so the two stores legitimately differ: TWA = same origin, iOS = bundled shell + API. This is still ONE web codebase (the app-shell is the same Livewire/Blade surface), just packaged two ways.

tensions.withLens: UI/visual design-system architect (density modes, dark mode, 16px input minimum)
tensions.tension: Mostly synergistic, but three frictions for the wrapped app: (a) the design system is light-first with dark gated behind a class toggle, yet a native app on iOS/Android is expected to honor the OS dark-mode setting and a status bar that matches — a class-only toggle won't auto-follow prefers-color-scheme in the WebView. (b) The 16px form-input minimum is framed as an iOS Safari zoom fix, but inside a WKWebView/Custom Tab you ALSO need viewport-fit=cover + safe-area-inset padding or the bottom tab nav collides with the home indicator. (c) compact dashboard density + thumb-tappable slot grid must still satisfy a 44x44pt native tap-target expectation, which 'compact' can violate.
tensions.reconciliation: (a) Keep light-first for public SEO pages, but make the dashboard app-shell respect prefers-color-scheme via the existing dark token set (the toggle just overrides), and drive theme-color / status-bar style from the active theme so the wrapped chrome matches — zero extra public-page cost. (b) Add viewport-fit=cover and env(safe-area-inset-*) padding tokens to the app-shell top bar and bottom nav as first-class spacing tokens (they're inert on web, load-bearing in the wrapper). (c) Enforce a hard 44x44pt minimum hit area on interactive controls in the compact slot grid via padding/expanded-touch tokens even when visual density is tight — visual compactness, generous hit area.

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer (manifest/icons/SW must not regress Lighthouse)
tensions.tension: My lens needs a COMPLETE manifest now (name, icons at all densities including maskable + 512px, theme_color, background_color, display:standalone, start_url, scope, shortcuts, screenshots) because Bubblewrap/PWABuilder read those exact fields to generate the native projects. The perf lens enforces a tight image/asset budget and a per-route byte budget.
