A full maskable-icon + screenshots set is real bytes, and an incomplete manifest fails the Lighthouse 'installable' PWA checks — so an under-built manifest hurts the score while an over-built one (huge screenshots eagerly referenced) could bloat.
tensions.reconciliation: The manifest and its icon/screenshot assets are referenced, not render-blocking, and are not on any route's critical path — so they cost ~0 Lighthouse perf but are required for installability credit AND for packaging. Ship the COMPLETE manifest now (it's tiny JSON), self-host icons as optimized PNG/AVIF at the required sizes (192, 512, maskable), keep manifest screenshots out of the LCP/critical path (they load only in the install UI), and add them to the asset budget as a separate 'install assets' bucket not counted against per-route page weight. Net: full app-readiness with no route-score regression.

finalRequirements: [must] Two separate routing surfaces, one codebase, app-shell only on the authenticated side: Public discovery/venue/booking stay independent SSR multi-page documents (each a clean, indexable, deep-linkable https URL). The Livewire dashboard+admin behind /dashboard and /admin are a persistent app-shell SPA (static top bar, bottom tab nav, content region, wire:navigate, zero full-page reloads inside the shell). Both surfaces share design tokens but are routed and packaged separately. The web PWA is the product; native shells add only deltas behind capability detection — never a forked UI.

[must] Complete, packaging-ready Web App Manifest shipped in v1: Include name, short_name, id, start_url (pointing at the dashboard/login, NOT the marketing homepage, so the installed app opens to the operator tool), scope, display:standalone, orientation, theme_color, background_color, lang:id, dir, self-hosted icons at 192/512 + a maskable 512 icon, app shortcuts (Today's bookings, New walk-in, Credit top-up), and store screenshots. These are the exact fields Bubblewrap/PWABuilder consume to generate the native projects; they sit off every route's critical path so they add ~0 Lighthouse perf cost (tracked in a separate 'install assets' budget bucket).

[must] Android TWA readiness via Digital Asset Links: Host /.well-known/assetlinks.json at the root with relation delegate_permission/common.handle_all_urls, target namespace android_app, the production package name, and the release SHA-256 signing fingerprint(s), so a Bubblewrap build verifies instantly and the URL bar is hidden (a TWA failing DAL verification shows browser chrome and fails review). Android TWA legitimately points at the same live origin that scores Lighthouse 99 — it is real Chrome, not a thin wrapper. Add the DAL file and a 'no-Apple-4.2-thin-wrapper' check to acceptance criteria.

[must] iOS path = bundled Capacitor shell + versioned API, NOT a remote-origin WebView: Do NOT ship iOS as Capacitor server.url pointing at the live site (production-discouraged and the classic Guideline 4.2 'thin web wrapper' rejection). Instead bundle the app-shell web build locally, talk to Laravel via a versioned JSON/Sanctum (Bearer-token) API for data, use Capacitor OTA (e.g. Cap-go/CapacitorUpdater) for fast web-layer updates without re-review, set limitsNavigationsToAppBoundDomains appropriately, and add genuine native value (push, biometric unlock, offline booking queue, share sheet, secure storage) to clear Apple's minimum-functionality bar. The web app-shell is the same Livewire/Blade surface — one codebase, packaged two ways (TWA same-origin, iOS bundled+API).

[must] Wrapper-safe auth & session that survives Custom Tabs and WKWebView: Use SameSite=Lax (not Strict) + Secure + HttpOnly for the session cookie so OTP/redirect handoffs through Chrome Custom Tabs keep the session; keep CSRF on all state-changing web routes for the browser/TWA path; for the iOS bundled app use the token/Sanctum API (Bearer) for state-changing actions so CSRF stays intact on web while the WebView carries a token. Make 'auth round-trips successfully inside both a TWA Custom Tab and a Capacitor WKWebView' an explicit acceptance gate, not a desktop-Chrome assumption. Extend connect-src/form-action in the CSP to include the bundled app origin (capacitor://localhost, the TWA host) without loosening script-src.

[must] Surface- and method-aware, self-healing service worker (no auth/tenant leakage, no frozen wrapped shell): The SW must NEVER cache /livewire/update, /dashboard/*, /admin/*, any non-GET request, or any response carrying Set-Cookie or Cache-Control: private — those go straight to network. Apply precache + stale-while-revalidate ONLY to public SSR routes, hashed Vite assets, self-hosted fonts, and the static-map facade image (clearly time-stamped; bookings always re-validate server-side via the EXCLUDE constraint). Name caches by build id, delete stale caches on activate, and gate skipWaiting/clients.claim behind an in-app 'update available' prompt so an installed/wrapped app can never get stuck on a stale shell. Let Livewire own intra-shell navigation; the SW owns only the offline fallback + static shell.

[must] Stable, app-mappable deep-link/universal-link scheme defined now: Define canonical https URL patterns (/{sport}/{city}/{venue-slug}, /venue/{slug}, /venue/{slug}/book, /dashboard/bookings/{id}, etc.) that round-trip identically in browser, Android TWA (handle_all_urls), and iOS (Universal Links via /.well-known/apple-app-site-association once iOS ships) so the same link opens the right screen everywhere with no redesign. Reserve and document the apple-app-site-association path now even if the iOS app lands later.

[should] Push + reminder layer designed as a swappable token source: Model push subscriptions abstractly (subscription/token + channel) so the existing WhatsApp/email reminder pipeline and a future native FCM (Android) / APNs (iOS, via Capacitor) are just additional delivery channels off the same notifications layer — adding native push later is a token-source swap, not a backend redesign.

[should] App-readiness UI/UX baked into the design system, not bolted on: Add viewport-fit=cover + env(safe-area-inset-*) padding tokens to the app-shell top bar and bottom nav (inert on web, load-bearing in the wrapper); honor prefers-color-scheme in the dashboard so the wrapped app follows OS dark mode and drives theme-color/status-bar style; enforce a hard 44x44pt minimum hit area on slot-grid and nav controls even in compact density; add lightweight web install/handoff UX with attribution that distinguishes web vs TWA vs iOS so later store apps inherit a warm audience.
missedByOthers: Nobody addressed that the installed-app start_url should be the DASHBOARD, not the public homepage — the public SSR site is what you deep-link INTO and what ranks, but the installed 'app' is the operator tool. The spec's PWA section (7.3) treats the PWA as one undifferentiated 'app shell'; this conflates the SEO surface and the app surface and will produce a confused install experience and a wrong manifest.

The service-worker strategy in spec 7.3 (stale-while-revalidate for discovery/availability) is described only for SEO/perf and is dangerously underspecified for SECURITY and for WRAPPED apps: there is no rule excluding /livewire/update, authenticated dashboard HTML, or private/Set-Cookie responses from the cache, and no self-healing/build-id cache-busting. As written it risks caching one tenant's dashboard and serving it to the next session, and bricking an installed app on a stale shell. This is a cross-cutting gap the security lens flagged for XSS/tenancy but nobody connected to the SW itself.

The spec assumes a single Lighthouse/PWA story but never states the iOS reality: iOS Safari does not support installable PWA manifests/SW the way Android does, and Apple's 4.2 gate rejects thin web wrappers. Without committing NOW to the bundled-Capacitor-shell + API + OTA + native-value path, 'release a mobile app fast later' is a false promise on iOS specifically. None of the other lenses (or the spec) name this iOS-specific divergence.

No lens covered the Sanctum/token API requirement implied by a bundled iOS shell: if iOS is bundled+API (not cookie+CSRF over the live origin), the booking/dashboard mutations need a token-auth API surface designed from v1, or the iOS app becomes a costly retrofit. This intersects the security lens (CSRF) but the API-for-app requirement itself is unaddressed.

apple-app-site-association and the connect-src CSP allowance for the app origin (capacitor://localhost) are concrete files/headers nobody listed; deep links and the native bridge silently fail without them, and they're trivial to add now and painful to retrofit.

Offline write-intent: a wrapped operator app in a dim venue back-office with flaky wifi needs at least an offline QUEUE for walk-in entry / status changes that re-validates server-side on reconnect (the EXCLUDE constraint is the integrity backstop). The spec's offline story is read-only (cached shell + stamped availability); for an installed operator tool, offline-read-only is a credible support complaint nobody flagged.

crossOut.lens: Web performance engineer — Lighthouse >=99 (mobile) on EVERY route, not just homepage

tensions.withLens: UI / visual design-system architect
tensions.tension: The design system mandates Plus Jakarta Sans self-hosted as a brand+UI typeface across BOTH surfaces. A 2-axis (weight) Latin+Latin-Ext subset of Plus Jakarta Sans variable woff2 is ~28-45KB; if multiple static weights are shipped instead (e.g. 400/500/600/700 = 4 files) it blows the critical font budget and each weight that paints late is a font-swap CLS + a render-blocking-equivalent on the LCP path. Plus Jakarta Sans is ALSO geometric with relatively wide metrics, so the 'metric-matched system fallback' is non-trivial — a sloppy fallback (size-adjust/ascent-override left at defaults) produces exactly the CLS=0-killing reflow on font swap that both of us are trying to avoid. The headline/marketing 'reads larger' requirement also pushes the LCP text element bigger, which on a cold-cache fold paints with the fallback first and reflows on swap.
tensions.reconciliation: Ship ONE variable woff2, Latin + Latin-Extended subset only (drop Vietnamese/Cyrillic/symbols ranges — Bahasa Indonesia needs no special diacritics beyond Latin-1), weight axis 400-700, target <=40KB; <link rel=preload as=font crossorigin> it in <head> on every route. Mandate font-display:swap PLUS a precisely-computed @font-face fallback override (size-adjust, ascent-override, descent-override, line-gap-override) measured against the actual Plus Jakarta Sans metrics so the system-font first paint occupies the IDENTICAL box — this makes the swap visually near-invisible AND keeps CLS contribution from font swap at literally 0.000. I will own a CI check that fails if the font subset exceeds 40KB or if more than one font file loads on a public route. The LCP element on public pages should be the hero IMAGE (preloaded AVIF), not a text headline, so font swap is off the LCP critical path entirely; if a route's LCP is unavoidably text, the fallback-metric override is what protects it.

tensions.withLens: UI / visual design-system architect
tensions.tension: The design system asks for shadow/radius/elevation tokens, an accent 'court/sport' color, status colors for 5 booking states, two density modes, and dark-mode-ready tokens — all expressed as Tailwind v4 @theme tokens. Two real perf risks: (1) defining lots of @theme tokens that are never used still costs nothing after purge, BUT the dark-mode token layer + density-mode class variants can double the generated CSS surface if both are emitted unconditionally, threatening the ~14KB-critical / single-bundle budget; (2) 'soft/comfortable' multi-layer box-shadows and large blur radii are genuinely expensive to paint and repaint and can drag TBT/INP on the slot-grid where many shadowed cards animate/refresh.
tensions.reconciliation: Agree on the shadow discipline explicitly: cap elevation at a 2-step scale, single-layer shadows, blur radius <= 8px, no shadow on any element that re-renders frequently (slot cells, Livewire-refreshed rows) — use a 1px border + bg tint for those instead of a shadow. For CSS budget: Tailwind v4's @theme tokens are free until referenced, so dark mode is fine as a token layer; the cost is the EMITTED utilities. Gate dark mode behind a .dark class that is NEVER present in the public-page DOM, and ensure the public critical CSS is extracted per-template so dark variants and dashboard-density utilities are simply absent from the public inline CSS. I will own a per-route critical-CSS byte budget (public route critical CSS <= 14KB) and a total-purged-CSS budget; the design system stays the single source of truth, but the build proves only the used subset ships per route.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer
tensions.tension: The security lens (rightly) requires a strict CSP with NO 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval' on script-src on both SSR and Livewire/Alpine pages. This directly collides with two of my fastest tools: (a) inlining critical CSS is fine (style-src) but inlining tiny bootstrap JS (e.g. the SW-register snippet, the LCP-image-priority hint, a click-to-load map facade handler) would normally be an inline <script> — blocked without 'unsafe-inline'; (b) Alpine 3 historically needed 'unsafe-eval' for its expression evaluation (x-data expressions are eval'd), and Livewire bundles Alpine. A nonce-per-request also fights HTML caching: a static-cacheable SSR page can't carry a per-request nonce, so a strict nonce-CSP can force public pages to become dynamic and lose full-page caching, raising TTFB and capping LCP.
tensions.reconciliation: Three concrete moves: (1) Use Alpine's CSP-friendly build (@alpinejs/csp) so Alpine no longer needs 'unsafe-eval' — this is the documented Livewire/Alpine path; it requires writing Alpine logic as methods not inline expressions, which the dashboard components must adopt from day one. (2) Eliminate inline <script> entirely: move the SW-register, map-facade, and install-prompt handlers into ONE tiny hashed external JS file (<5KB, deferred) referenced by hash in script-src — no nonce needed, fully cacheable, CSP-clean. (3) For the public SSR pages keep them nonce-FREE and fully page-cacheable by using hash-based CSP (script-src 'self' + sha256 hashes of the few static inline blocks if any survive) so static HTML caching and low TTFB are preserved; reserve per-request nonces only for the authenticated dashboard where pages are already dynamic and uncacheable anyway. Net: strict CSP holds, zero inline-script perf workaround, and public-page cacheability/TTFB is untouched.

tensions.withLens: PWA + mobile-app packaging engineer
tensions.tension: We agree the public site stays SSR/multi-page and the dashboard is an app-shell SPA via wire:navigate. The tension is the SERVICE WORKER: an app-shell SW that aggressively pre-caches and serves a cached shell is great for the wrapped dashboard, but if that same SW scope or caching strategy touches the PUBLIC SSR routes it will (a) serve a stale shell that fails SEO freshness and can serve outdated availability, and (b) risk caching authenticated/POST responses. The packaging lens also wants 'offline app-shell' broadly; an over-broad precache inflates install cost and a frozen SW becomes a stale-content support nightmare — and a stale SW serving an old hashed-CSS/JS manifest will silently regress my Lighthouse 99 in the field even though CI was green.
tensions.reconciliation: Define explicit SW route policies that satisfy both: NEVER cache (network-only, never store) for /livewire/update, any POST, any Set-Cookie/auth response, and the booking/availability JSON — this also satisfies security's 'no auth/POST cached'. Public SSR HTML = network-first with a short SWR window and a visible timestamp on availability data (bookings always re-validate server-side via the EXCLUDE constraint anyway). Static hashed assets (CSS/JS/font/AVIF) = cache-first immutable (they're content-hashed, so a deploy changes the URL and can't go stale). App-shell precache = ONLY the dashboard shell skeleton, scoped so it never serves public routes. Mandatory: a versioned SW with skipWaiting + clients.claim on a controlled update flow and a 'new version available' prompt, plus a kill-switch, so an installed/wrapped app can never get stuck on a stale shell that regresses Lighthouse in the field. I will add a Lighthouse-CI run against the SW-served (warm) state, not just cold, so a bad caching policy fails the gate.

tensions.withLens: PWA + mobile-app packaging engineer
tensions.tension: The packaging lens wants the dashboard structured as a persistent app-shell SPA (static top bar + bottom tab nav + content region) driven by wire:navigate with no full-page reloads — to feel native and survive wrapping. My risk: a persistent shell that holds long-lived Livewire/Alpine state across many navigations is exactly where TBT/INP and memory creep accumulate on low-end Android (the Lighthouse mobile target). Each wire:navigate swaps the content region but @persist'd chrome + accumulated event listeners + un-GC'd Alpine components can grow main-thread work, so the SHELL can pass Lighthouse on first load yet degrade after 10 navigations — which CI's single-load audit won't catch.
tensions.reconciliation: Keep the persistent shell but bound it: @persist ONLY the top bar and bottom nav (static, no heavy state), and let wire:navigate fully tear down and rebuild the content-region component on each navigation so listeners/Alpine instances are GC'd (Livewire 3 does this by default on navigate unless @persist'd — so the rule is 'persist chrome, never persist content'). Hold the dashboard JS budget at <=80KB (Livewire+Alpine core) and forbid per-route JS additions inside the shell. Add a CI assertion that audits a dashboard route AFTER several wire:navigate hops (scripted) so TBT/INP is measured in the steady-state SPA condition, not just cold load — this is the only way 'app-shell' and '99 mobile' are both true in practice.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer
tensions.tension: Security requires security headers, CSP, and verified webhooks — all good — but a strict, large CSP/Permissions-Policy/HSTS header set is sent on EVERY response including the page-cached public HTML, and an over-eager Permissions-Policy or COOP/COEP can break the click-to-load map facade (cross-origin isolation) or the future TWA. More importantly, if CSP enforcement is implemented as Laravel middleware that runs per-request, it can defeat full-page HTTP caching of public SSR pages (the response is assembled dynamically), raising TTFB and capping LCP < 2.5s on cold routes.
tensions.reconciliation: Emit the static security headers (CSP via hashes, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy) at the EDGE / web-server layer (or via a cached response with headers baked in) so they cost zero per-request app time and don't block full-page caching — the CSP for public pages is static (hash-based, no per-request nonce) precisely so it CAN be set once and cached. Keep Permissions-Policy minimal (geolocation=(self) for radius search, nothing that breaks the map facade); do NOT enable COEP/COOP cross-origin isolation since we self-host everything and it offers no benefit while risking the facade/wrapper. This gives security its full header set with no TTFB/LCP penalty on the routes that must hit 99.

finalRequirements: [must] PER-ROUTE LIGHTHOUSE CI GATE AT 0.99 (replaces spec's >=98). Lighthouse CI (lhci autorun, mobile/Moto-G-class throttling) must audit EVERY public route CLASS — home, /{sport}/{city} discovery list, /venue/{slug}, /venue/{slug}/book, plus a representative authenticated dashboard route — asserting performance, accessibility, best-practices, AND seo each >= 0.99. Any route < 0.99 fails the pipeline. Audit the dashboard route in TWO states: cold load AND after >=5 scripted wire:navigate hops (steady-state SPA), because the app-shell can pass cold and degrade after navigation. Also run one audit against the SW-warmed (returning-visitor) state so a bad cache policy can't silently regress the field score.

[must] PER-TEMPLATE ASSET BUDGETS, CI-ENFORCED (lhci budgets.json + bundle check). Public SSR routes: <=30KB JS (gzip/brotli), <=14KB critical CSS inlined + deferred remainder, LCP image <=90KB AVIF, ONE font file <=40KB (variable, Latin+Latin-Ext subset). Dashboard: <=80KB JS (Livewire+Alpine core) and per-Livewire-component HTML payload <=40KB. Hard targets on every route: TBT < 150ms, CLS = 0 (every dynamic element reserves its box), LCP < 2.5s throttled. A budget breach fails CI — '99 everywhere' is only real if every route is measured.

[must] LCP DISCIPLINE = PRELOADED SELF-HOSTED AVIF WITH EXPLICIT DIMENSIONS, FONT OFF THE LCP PATH. All venue/court imagery served as responsive AVIF (WebP fallback) at multiple widths via /srcset, every carrying intrinsic width/height, self-hosted, no CDN. The LCP element per public route is a preloaded image (fetchpriority=high, ), not a text headline, so Plus Jakarta Sans swap is never on the LCP critical path. Below-fold images lazy-loaded; the map is a static AVIF facade (click-to-load), never eager map JS on the LCP/TBT path.

[must] ZERO RENDER-BLOCKING + FONT-SWAP-CLS=0 (joint with design-system lens). Inline per-template critical CSS, defer the rest; ship exactly ONE Plus Jakarta Sans variable woff2 (Latin + Latin-Extended subset, weight axis 400-700, <=40KB), preloaded with crossorigin, font-display:swap, AND a metric-matched @font-face fallback with measured size-adjust/ascent-override/descent-override/line-gap-override so the system-font first paint occupies an identical box and font swap contributes 0.000 to CLS. CI fails if >1 font file loads on a public route or the subset exceeds 40KB.

[must] CSP THAT COSTS ZERO PERF (joint with security lens): NO inline scripts anywhere — consolidate SW-register, map-facade, and install-prompt into one hashed, deferred external JS file (<5KB) so script-src needs neither 'unsafe-inline' nor a per-request nonce on public pages. Adopt Alpine's CSP build (@alpinejs/csp) so the dashboard needs no 'unsafe-eval'. Public-page CSP is hash-based and STATIC, set at the edge/web-server with the other security headers (HSTS, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(self)), so it never blocks full-page HTTP caching or raises TTFB; per-request nonces are reserved for the already-dynamic authenticated dashboard only. No COEP/COOP (everything self-hosted; it would risk the map facade and the wrapper).

[must] SERVICE-WORKER ROUTE POLICY MATRIX (joint with PWA + security lenses). network-only / never-store for /livewire/update, all POST, all Set-Cookie/auth responses, and booking/availability JSON. Public SSR HTML = network-first + short SWR with a visible 'availability as of HH:MM' timestamp (server re-validates via the EXCLUDE constraint regardless). Content-hashed static assets (CSS/JS/font/AVIF) = cache-first immutable. App-shell precache = dashboard shell skeleton ONLY, scoped so it never serves public SSR routes. Versioned SW with controlled update (skipWaiting + clients.claim + 'update available' prompt + kill-switch) so a wrapped/installed app can never get stuck on a stale shell that regresses Lighthouse or serves stale availability.

[must] PERSISTENT APP-SHELL WITHOUT MAIN-THREAD CREEP (joint with PWA lens). @persist ONLY the static top bar and bottom-tab nav; wire:navigate must fully tear down and rebuild the content-region component each navigation (never @persist content) so Alpine instances and event listeners are GC'd and TBT/INP/memory stay flat across the session on low-end Android. No per-route JS added inside the shell; dashboard JS frozen at the 80KB core budget.

[should] CHEAP-PAINT VISUALS ON HOT SURFACES (joint with design-system lens). Cap elevation at a 2-step single-layer shadow scale, blur <= 8px, and FORBID box-shadow on any frequently re-rendered element (slot-grid cells, Livewire-refreshed table rows) — use a 1px border + bg tint instead — so the most-used, most-data-dense surface (booking calendar / dashboard calendar) doesn't accrue paint/INP cost. Dark-mode and dashboard-density utilities must be absent from public-page critical CSS (gated behind .dark / density class never present in the public DOM).

[should] TTFB/LCP FLOOR ON ALL ROUTES. Full-page HTTP cache + Redis hot-availability cache so public SSR TTFB stays low and stable enough that LCP < 2.5s throttled on warm AND cold for every route class. Public CSP/headers are static so caching is never defeated. Discovery list and venue page in particular must not let an interactive map, review list, or gallery push eager JS onto the TBT path or cause CLS as images/cards load; schema.org AggregateRating present on the venue page for SEO=99 without a layout-shift cost.

[nice] APP-READINESS AT ZERO LIGHTHOUSE COST. The same routes, manifest, hashed assets, and SW that score 99 on web are exactly what the TWA (Bubblewrap) and iOS Capacitor wrappers load — no wrapper-specific bundle, no parallel codebase, no Lighthouse regression when wrapped. The steady-state-SPA and SW-warmed CI audits above are what prove the wrapped experience also stays at 99.
missedByOthers: NO ONE OWNS THE 'EVERY ROUTE' MEASUREMENT — the current spec says 'Lighthouse >=98 on KEY public pages'. 'Key pages' is the single most dangerous phrase for the owner's '99 on EVERY page/section' hard requirement: it lets the discovery list, venue page, booking calendar, and dashboard ship unmeasured. The gate must be a per-route-CLASS matrix at 0.99, not a homepage check. Without this the other lenses' good work is unverifiable in the field.

THE DASHBOARD/APP-SHELL IS NEVER LIGHTHOUSE-AUDITED IN THE SPEC. The PWA lens designs the app-shell and the spec lists a Lighthouse gate, but no one specifies auditing an AUTHENTICATED route, and critically no one specifies auditing it in STEADY-STATE (after several wire:navigate hops) or SW-WARMED. The app-shell that 'feels native' is precisely where TBT/INP/memory degrade over a session — a cold single-load audit will miss it and the wrapped store app will feel janky despite a green CI.

CLS FROM LIVEWIRE PARTIAL RE-RENDERS IS UNADDRESSED. When the slot grid refreshes after a date change or a hold timer tick, the Livewire DOM diff can resize/reflow the grid and the booking widget — a classic CLS source on the highest-value interactive route. The booking calendar must reserve fixed box dimensions for the slot grid, the price, and the hold-timer region so a Livewire round-trip never shifts layout. No lens flagged that Livewire re-renders are a CLS vector.

THE 16px-MIN-INPUT RULE IS ALSO A PERF/UX SEO SIGNAL, NOT JUST UX. The design lens correctly mandates 16px min on inputs to stop iOS zoom-on-focus — but this also feeds Lighthouse's accessibility/best-practices and the 'tap targets' / viewport audits that gate the 0.99 on the SEO and accessibility categories (which the owner requires at 99 too, not just performance). Worth stating that the 0.99 gate is ALL FOUR categories, so accessibility tap-target and contrast tokens are perf-budget-relevant, not separable.

IMAGE PIPELINE NEEDS A SERVER-SIDE GENERATION + CACHE STRATEGY, not just a format mandate. Venue-uploaded photos arrive as arbitrary JPEGs; 'serve AVIF at multiple widths' implies an on-upload (or first-request-then-cache) transcode pipeline with pre-computed intrinsic dimensions stored in the DB so the can carry width/height with zero runtime measurement. Without the dimensions stored, the SSR can't emit explicit width/height and CLS breaks. This is an architecture requirement the data-model lens didn't capture (no width/height columns on a venue_images table).

DEFERRED THIRD-PARTY/ANALYTICS WILL QUIETLY KILL TBT. The spec is 'zero external CDN' which is excellent for me, but any future analytics, chat widget, or WhatsApp click-to-chat SDK dropped onto public pages later is the classic 99->85 regression. Lock a policy now: no third-party JS on public SSR routes ever; analytics must be a first-party, hashed, deferred beacon — otherwise the 0.99 gate will be 'temporarily disabled' the first time marketing wants a pixel.

PWA INSTALLABILITY AUDIT IS A BEST-PRACTICES/PWA SCORE INPUT. To hold best-practices at 0.99 and to satisfy the packaging lens's TWA/Bubblewrap dependency on a complete manifest, the manifest completeness (icons incl. maskable 512px, theme/background color, display:standalone, start_url, scope) must be CI-validated (e.g. pwa/manifest checks) — a half-complete manifest both fails Bubblewrap verification AND drags the best-practices category.

crossOut.lens: Application Security Engineer (OWASP Top 10 + Laravel 12 hardening + payments/OTP/multi-tenancy + Indonesia UU PDP)

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer — strict CSP (my own requirement) vs UI design-system 'inline critical CSS' + Web-perf 'inline critical CSS, defer the rest' + PWA 'Alpine inline expressions in app-shell'
tensions.tension: My hard requirement is a strict CSP with NO 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval' on script-src. But (a) the design-system + web-perf lenses both REQUIRE inlining ~14KB critical CSS for Lighthouse 99, and (b) Livewire 3's own CSP-safe build (confirmed in current docs) needs script-src 'nonce-[random]' 'strict-dynamic' AND style-src 'unsafe-inline'. A naive 'lock everything down' CSP breaks both Lighthouse and Livewire/Alpine. Conversely, the common shortcut of 'unsafe-inline'+'unsafe-eval' on script-src to make Alpine 'just work' destroys my XSS guarantee.
tensions.reconciliation: Split the policy by directive and surface, and accept ONE deliberate, bounded relaxation. SCRIPT-SRC stays strict everywhere: 'self' 'nonce-{perRequestNonce}' 'strict-dynamic', NO unsafe-inline, NO unsafe-eval. Use Livewire 3's CSP-safe build (it ships an evaluator that avoids new Function/eval) and Alpine.data()/x-bind component registrations for any non-trivial expression — pushed into nonce'd <script> blocks, never inline x-on handlers with complex logic. STYLE-SRC: accept 'self' 'unsafe-inline' ONLY for styles (this is the documented Livewire requirement and is low-risk because injected CSS cannot exfiltrate without a connect-src/img-src sink, which we also lock down). This lets the design-system inline its 14KB critical CSS AND keeps Lighthouse 99 with zero script-XSS exposure. Lock down the real exfiltration sinks instead: connect-src 'self' https://*.xendit.co, img-src 'self' data:, frame-ancestors 'none', base-uri 'self', form-action 'self', object-src 'none', plus Trusted Types where supported. Net: the perf/design lenses keep inline CSS; I keep an XSS-resistant script policy. This must be ONE shared header pipeline (middleware) so the SSR public surface and the Livewire dashboard get the same baseline, differing only by nonce injection.

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer — Lighthouse 99 'best-practices' category vs my full security-header set + my OTP rate-limit error UX
tensions.tension: Lighthouse's best-practices audit rewards HTTPS, CSP, and no console errors, so most of my headers HELP the score — but two collisions exist: (1) an over-broad or malformed CSP that Lighthouse flags as 'allows unsafe-inline' actually DOCKS best-practices points, so a sloppy security header can fail the 99 gate; (2) my OTP/booking abuse controls (rate limits, hold-spam throttles) can surface as 4xx/JS errors or layout-shifting error banners on exactly the high-risk routes (booking calendar) the web-perf lens needs at CLS=0 and TBT<150ms.
tensions.reconciliation: (1) Treat CSP as a Lighthouse asset, not a liability: ship the strict nonce-based CSP above (Lighthouse's CSP-XSS audit specifically rewards 'strict-dynamic' + nonce and penalizes unsafe-inline on script-src), so security and the best-practices 99 move together. Add the security headers to the Lighthouse CI assertMatrix as a positive assert. (2) For abuse controls on perf-critical routes: render rate-limit/lock states as pre-reserved, fixed-height inline UI regions (the design-system already mandates reserved boxes / CLS=0), return throttle responses as Livewire-friendly 422 with a body the component renders in-place — never a full error page, never a late-injected banner. OTP cooldowns are SSR'd as a disabled button with a server-rendered countdown placeholder so there is zero layout shift and zero extra JS. Security UX and CLS=0 are satisfied together.

tensions.withLens: PWA + mobile-app packaging engineer — service worker / offline caching vs my 'never cache auth or POST /livewire/update' rule, AND TWA/Capacitor wrapper vs CSRF/SameSite cookies
tensions.tension: The PWA lens wants an aggressive app-shell SW (stale-while-revalidate, offline shell) and a TWA (Chrome Custom Tabs) + Capacitor (WKWebView) wrapper. Three security collisions: (a) a SW that caches authenticated HTML or any POST/livewire/update response leaks session-scoped data and can replay stale CSRF tokens — and the PWA lens itself names this as the #1 silent breakage; (b) wrapping in a native WebView changes the cookie/origin context — SameSite=Lax/Strict, Secure, and HttpOnly must still hold or the wrapper either can't auth or becomes CSRF-exposed; (c) Digital Asset Links (assetlinks.json) and deep links create new trusted-origin relationships that, if misconfigured, let a malicious app claim the origin or intercept booking/payment deep links.
tensions.reconciliation: (a) SW caching policy is security-owned: cache ONLY versioned, public, non-authenticated static assets + the public app-shell skeleton; NEVER cache responses to /livewire/update, any route under the auth middleware, or anything with a Set-Cookie/Authorization/Vary:Cookie header. Enforce with an explicit allowlist + a runtime guard that refuses to cache POST and any response whose request carried the session cookie. Network-only for all state-changing and authenticated requests; bookings always revalidate server-side (the spec's EXCLUDE constraint already guarantees integrity, so stale availability can NEVER produce a double-book). (b) Cookies stay HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Lax (Lax, not Strict, so the OTP/login redirect and Xendit return-URL top-level navigations keep the session); for the Capacitor WKWebView, serve the app from the real https://app.venuepro.asia origin (not capacitor://localhost) so SameSite/CSRF semantics are identical to web — this also satisfies the PWA lens's 'one origin' goal. Keep Laravel CSRF on all POST; Livewire's _token already rides every update. (c) assetlinks.json + apple-app-site-association are security-reviewed artifacts pinned to specific SHA-256 signing-cert fingerprints and a fixed app-id; deep-link handlers must AUTHORIZE the target (a booking/dashboard deep link still hits policies/Gate server-side — a deep link is a URL, not an authz bypass). I additionally require: no secrets in the manifest/wrapper, and the TWA fallback (failed DAL) must NOT expose a different, less-hardened origin.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer ↔ PWA lens — push notifications as a future PII/abuse surface
tensions.tension: The PWA lens designs a push subscription + delivery layer (Web Push now, FCM/APNs later) reusing the WhatsApp/email reminder pipeline. Push endpoints, VAPID keys, device tokens, and a 'send to subscriber' backend are a new injection/abuse/PII surface that no lens currently secures: token theft → spam/phishing as VenuePro, unauthenticated subscribe → push-bomb, and tokens are linkable to a person (UU PDP personal data).
tensions.reconciliation: Bake security into the push layer from v1 even though native ships later: (1) subscriptions are bound to an authenticated user + tenant and authorized on every send (a venue can only push to its own bookings' players); (2) VAPID/FCM/APNs server keys live in the same secrets vault as Xendit keys, never in the client/manifest; (3) push payloads carry NO PII/booking details in the encrypted-at-rest-only sense — minimal payload, fetch-on-open for specifics behind auth; (4) device tokens are personal data under UU PDP → included in retention, export, and erasure flows; (5) rate-limit subscribe + send. This makes the 'token-source swap' the PWA lens wants safe rather than a new breach vector.

tensions.withLens: UI / visual design-system architect & spec's own copy — 'hard, unmissable, prominent' refund/cancellation disclosures vs comfortable/airy UX and conversion
tensions.tension: The spec mandates a 'hard, unmissable disclosure' (refunds handled by venue, service fee non-refundable, etc.) on every transaction page before payment, and from a security/compliance + UU PDP consent standpoint I REQUIRE this to be a genuine, logged, affirmative consent — not a pre-checked box buried in fine print. The design lens optimizes for airy, low-friction, high-conversion booking. A purely 'comfortable' checkout could legally/operationally under-disclose; a wall-of-text legalese disclosure tanks conversion and arguably hurts the 'easy to use' requirement.
tensions.reconciliation: Make disclosure a first-class, token-styled component, not an afterthought: a compact, high-contrast summary card (using the design system's status-color + spacing tokens) stating the 3 load-bearing facts in plain Bahasa, with an UNCHECKED, explicit 'Saya mengerti' affirmation that must be actively toggled to enable Pay — server-side enforced, and the consent event (policy version + timestamp + user) written to the audit log. Full policy is one tap away (disclosure/accordion), not a wall of text. This satisfies my consent/UU-PDP-lawful-basis + non-repudiation need AND the design lens's clarity/comfort: clear beats cluttered. Critically, the affirmation gate is SERVER-enforced (booking cannot transition to a payable charge without the logged consent) so it is not a client-side cosmetic that conversion-optimization can quietly delete.

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer & SEO (SSR public discovery) — public, crawlable, low-friction discovery vs my enumeration / scraping / abuse controls
tensions.tension: SEO + perf REQUIRE the discovery/venue pages to be fully SSR, crawlable, fast, and friction-free (no login wall, no CAPTCHA on the LCP path, bot-accessible). My enumeration/scraping/abuse instincts (rate-limit everything, block bots, CAPTCHA) directly fight that: a CAPTCHA or aggressive WAF rule on /{sport}/{city} would kill both Lighthouse 99 and Googlebot indexing, and over-eager rate limiting on the public radius search degrades the very SEO surface that is the product's growth engine.
tensions.reconciliation: Separate the public READ surface from the state-changing/auth surface and apply controls asymmetrically. Public SSR discovery/venue pages: NO CAPTCHA, NO login wall, NO per-request JS challenge — keep them crawlable and 99. Protect them with cheap, non-blocking layers instead: aggressive HTTP caching/CDN-edge-equivalent (also a perf win), generous IP/ASN rate limits tuned ABOVE Googlebot + normal-user volume, and bot management that allowlists verified search-engine crawlers (reverse-DNS verified Googlebot/Bingbot) while throttling abusive scrapers — never a challenge that Lighthouse or a crawler would hit. Concentrate the strict controls (OTP rate limits, hold/booking throttles, CAPTCHA-on-suspicion) ONLY on the POST/auth/booking endpoints, which are not SEO-indexed and not on the Lighthouse LCP path. This is also how I keep OTP-abuse controls from ever touching the perf-critical public render.

tensions.withLens: PWA + web-perf — Xendit return-URL redirect + WhatsApp deep links vs my open-redirect / SSRF / phishing controls
tensions.tension: Booking flow round-trips through Xendit (success_return_url/failure_return_url) and out to WhatsApp/email reminders with deep links back into the app/TWA. The PWA lens wants these deep links to round-trip seamlessly into the right screen. That seamlessness is exactly where open-redirect, link-spoofing, and (server-side) SSRF on any venue-supplied URL (venue website, IG/WA 'powered by your funnel' links, image URLs) creep in.
tensions.reconciliation: Allowlist every redirect and deep-link target: return URLs are server-constructed from the booking id, never reflected from a query param; deep-link routing maps a fixed scheme to internal routes and refuses arbitrary destinations. Venue-supplied URLs (website, social, image) are validated, scheme-restricted (https only), never server-side-fetched against internal ranges (SSRF allowlist + block link-local/RFC1918/metadata IPs), and rendered with rel=noopener noreferrer. This keeps the PWA's smooth deep-link round-trip while closing the redirect/SSRF holes the seamlessness would otherwise open.

finalRequirements: [must] WEBHOOK IS THE ONLY MONEY MUTATOR — UNFORGEABLE, IDEMPOTENT, REPLAY-PROOF, TENANT-BOUND (corrects spec 6.1/6.4). Verify x-callback-token via hash_equals against the stored token (constant-time); CRITICAL version-accurate caveat: Xendit's x-callback-token is a STATIC shared token, NOT an HMAC over the body, so token-alone is insufficient — additionally bind every event by re-fetching/asserting the payment by provider_ref AND matching business_id/sub-account → our internal venue_id (reject if the event's sub-account != the booking's venue sub-account). INSERT event_id into webhook_events (UNIQUE) BEFORE side-effects for idempotency; enforce the allowed-transition matrix so out-of-order/stale events can't overwrite terminal states; re-validate amount_idr against the stored booking total (reject on mismatch → blocks amount-tampering). Webhook route is CSRF-exempt but IP-allowlisted to Xendit ranges, no auth-session, fast 200 + queued side-effects. Credit deduction and any venue refund trigger happen ONLY inside this verified, idempotent path.

[must] xenPlatform for-user-id CONFUSED-DEPUTY / CROSS-TENANT MONEY CONTROL (missed by all other lenses). Every Xendit call carries for-user-id = the venue's sub-account; the platform key can act on ANY sub-account. Centralize all Xendit calls in one gateway service that derives for-user-id strictly from the authorized, route-bound venue_id (never from request input), and assert the target sub-account belongs to the acting tenant before any charge/refund. A venue dashboard refund must be authorized by policy AND scoped so a venue can only refund its own payments; the master/platform API key is never exposed to tenant-triggerable arbitrary for-user-id. This is the highest-value money-IDOR and the spec currently doesn't name it.

[must] STRICT, SPLIT, NONCE-BASED CSP via shared middleware (reconciles with perf + design + Livewire). script-src 'self' 'nonce-{perRequest}' 'strict-dynamic' — NO unsafe-inline, NO unsafe-eval; use Livewire 3's CSP-safe build + Alpine.data() in nonce'd scripts. style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' is the ONE accepted relaxation (documented Livewire requirement; enables the design system's inlined 14KB critical CSS and keeps Lighthouse 99). Lock the exfiltration sinks: connect-src 'self' https://*.xendit.co; img-src 'self' data:; default-src 'self'; base-uri 'self'; form-action 'self'; frame-ancestors 'none'; object-src 'none'; upgrade-insecure-requests; Trusted Types where supported. Same baseline header pipeline for SSR public + Livewire dashboard; assert presence in Lighthouse CI (it raises best-practices, not lowers it).

[must] OTP / WhatsApp ABUSE — SMS-PUMPING, BRUTE-FORCE, ENUMERATION (composite-key, layered, constant-time). Per-phone + per-IP + per-device + global send-rate limits with exponential cooldowns and a daily cap; channel-cost circuit breaker (alert + auto-throttle on send-volume spikes = SMS-pumping defense). OTPs are 6-digit, single-use, short-TTL, hashed at rest, max N verify attempts then invalidate. Constant-time, identical responses for existing vs non-existing accounts (no enumeration). Throttle UI rendered as pre-reserved, server-rendered disabled-button + countdown placeholder (CLS=0, no extra JS) so abuse controls never break the booking-route Lighthouse 99. CAPTCHA-on-suspicion only on the POST OTP endpoint, never on SSR/LCP path.

[must] MULTI-TENANCY / IDOR — PROVABLE CROSS-VENUE ISOLATION (OWASP A01, aligns with spec 4/7.4). Route-bound active venue (never an implicit global scope, never venue_id from request body); every dashboard query and write filtered by the authorized venue_id; spatie policies on bookings/payments/credits/refunds/reviews/settings; platform-admin elevation via Gate::before ONLY, which must NOT leak into tenant read models (public discovery deliberately un-scoped, dashboard strictly scoped). Mandatory automated isolation tests: venue_staff/venue_owner of venue A get 403/404 on every venue B resource and money action; admin elevation cannot be assumed by a tenant token.

[must] MASS-ASSIGNMENT, VALIDATION, RAW-POSTGIS SQLi (OWASP A03/A08). No unguarded fill/update on privileged fields (status, venue_id, price_idr, service_fee_idr, balance, role, xendit_subaccount_id, rating_avg) — use explicit Form Requests / validated DTOs and never trust client-set money/status. All PostGIS radius + full-text queries use bound parameters / parameterized expressions — NEVER string-concatenate lat/lng/radius/search input (cast & validate numerics; ST_DWithin with bindings). Validate file uploads (venue images): mime+extension+magic-byte check, size cap, re-encode/strip EXIF, store off web-root with random names, served from a no-exec path; SSRF allowlist on any server-side fetch (https only, block RFC1918/link-local/169.254.169.254).

[must] PII / UU PDP (Law 27/2022) — PHONE NUMBERS & DATA-SUBJECT RIGHTS (compliance, fines up to 2% revenue; missed in spec beyond a one-line mention). Documented lawful basis (contract performance for bookings; explicit consent for marketing/push); encrypt phone numbers + booking history at rest, redact in logs; data-subject access/export/erasure flows; defined retention; 72-hour breach-notification runbook (detection → assess → notify regulator + subjects). Booking-disclosure consent (refunds-by-venue, fee non-refundable) captured as an affirmative, server-enforced, audit-logged event (policy version + timestamp) — satisfies lawful basis AND the spec's 'hard disclosure', rendered as a token-styled compact consent card (clarity > legalese, conversion-safe).

[must] BASELINE LARAVEL 12 HARDENING + WRAPPER-SAFE SESSION/CSRF (enables PWA/TWA/Capacitor safely). Cookies HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Lax (Lax keeps OTP-login + Xendit return-URL top-level redirects working); CSRF on all state-changing web/Livewire routes; security headers (HSTS preload, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy strict-origin-when-cross-origin, Permissions-Policy minimal, COOP/CORP). Service worker MUST cache ONLY versioned public static assets + public shell — NEVER /livewire/update, never auth-middleware routes, never any response that carried the session cookie or Set-Cookie (network-only for authenticated + all POST). Capacitor app served from the real https origin (not capacitor://localhost) so CSRF/SameSite semantics are identical to web. assetlinks.json + apple-app-site-association pinned to exact signing-cert SHA-256 + app-id; deep links re-authorize server-side (a deep link is a URL, not an authz bypass).

[should] BOOKING-FLOW BUSINESS-LOGIC ABUSE beyond the EXCLUDE constraint. Rate-limit hold creation per user/IP (anti hold-spam/inventory-griefing); cap concurrent active holds per user; ensure expired-hold release job is robust (no orphaned locks); credit-deduct and refund-credit-reversal are atomic, idempotent, and cannot be driven negative or double-spent by replayed/duplicate webhooks; walk-in path goes through the same EXCLUDE + authz (staff can't create bookings for other venues). 'Insufficient credits → online bookings paused' must fail safe (never silently overbook free).

[should] AUDIT LOGGING + SECRETS/SUPPLY-CHAIN. Tamper-evident, queryable, append-only audit trail for money + security events (login/OTP, role/permission change, credit topup/deduct/adjust, refund executed, consent given, webhook processed, admin elevation) — proves who did what, excludes raw OTPs/tokens/PII. Xendit master + sub-account keys, x-callback-token, WA BSP keys, DB/Redis creds, APP_KEY, VAPID keys in a vault/encrypted env, rotated, never in client/manifest/repo; key/dependency CVE scanning (composer audit + Livewire/Xendit SDK pinning) in CI.
missedByOthers: XENPLATFORM for-user-id IS THE #1 UNNAMED MONEY-IDOR: the platform's master Xendit key can act on ANY venue sub-account via the for-user-id header. No other lens (and the spec) names this confused-deputy surface. A single gateway that derives for-user-id ONLY from the route-bound, authorized venue_id — never from request input — is mandatory, or one compromised/buggy dashboard action can charge/refund another venue's account.

XENDIT x-callback-token IS A STATIC SHARED SECRET, NOT A PER-MESSAGE HMAC SIGNATURE (confirmed against current Xendit docs). The spec says 'signature-verified webhooks', which overstates the guarantee. Token-equality alone is replayable and doesn't bind to the payload, so the design MUST add: idempotency via UNIQUE event_id, amount re-validation against the stored booking, and sub-account/business_id→venue_id binding. Without this, a leaked token (or a misrouted event) could flip the wrong booking to paid.

WEBHOOK→AUTOMATIC VENUE REFUND IS A FRAUD/DOS VECTOR: spec 6.1 step 4 says a late/0-row payment 'triggers a venue refund' automatically. An attacker who can spoof or replay 'paid' events could drain a venue's Xendit balance via forced refunds. The refund trigger must sit behind the same unforgeable+idempotent gate AND a sanity check (only refund a payment that actually settled to that sub-account, exactly once).

PUSH-NOTIFICATION LAYER IS AN UNSECURED FUTURE PII/ABUSE SURFACE: the PWA lens designs it but no one secures it. Device tokens are UU PDP personal data; unauthenticated subscribe = push-bomb; stolen VAPID/FCM keys = phishing as VenuePro. Must be auth+tenant-bound, minimal-payload, key-vaulted, and in the erasure/retention scope from v1.

CONSENT/DISCLOSURE MUST BE SERVER-ENFORCED & AUDIT-LOGGED, NOT COSMETIC: the spec's 'hard disclosure' and conversion optimization can quietly degrade into a client-side pre-checked box. For UU PDP lawful basis + non-repudiation it must be an affirmative, server-gated, versioned, logged consent event — otherwise it's neither compliant nor legally protective for the platform.

SW CACHE POISONING OF AUTH/POST: the perf and PWA lenses both want an aggressive service worker; neither states the security rule that the SW must NEVER cache /livewire/update, authenticated HTML, or anything carrying the session cookie — the single most common way a wrapped Livewire PWA leaks session data or replays stale CSRF tokens.

PUBLIC SSR DISCOVERY vs ABUSE CONTROLS asymmetry is unstated: protecting the crawlable, Lighthouse-99 public surface requires NON-blocking, crawler-aware controls (no CAPTCHA/login wall on the LCP path) while concentrating strict limits on POST/auth/booking. Getting this wrong silently kills either SEO/Lighthouse or security.

SSRF / OPEN-REDIRECT via Xendit return-URLs + venue-supplied URLs (website/IG/WA/image) + deep links: the 'powered by your funnel' and 'seamless deep-link round-trip' features introduce reflected redirects and server-side fetches that must be allowlisted/scheme-restricted/internal-IP-blocked — not mentioned by any lens.

crossOut.lens: UX — usability, accessibility, conversion, and the actual booking-flow ergonomics across the public SSR site and the Livewire dashboard PWA. My job is the human-facing layer: can a Jakarta player on a mid-range Android complete discover -> book -> pay in under a minute without confusion, can venue staff run the calendar fast, and is the whole thing accessible (WCAG AA), forgiving of errors, and trustworthy at the moment money moves. I sit downstream of every other lens because their constraints (perf budgets, CSP, design tokens, app-shell, security gates) all land as friction or comfort in the user's hands.

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer (Lighthouse >=99 every route)
tensions.tension: The perf lens treats the booking calendar and discovery list as the highest-risk routes for TBT/CLS and wants Livewire payloads minimal and 'no eager JS.' But UX-critical affordances cost exactly the things they're budgeting against: a real-time hold countdown timer, optimistic slot-press feedback within 100ms, inline-validation on the OTP/phone field, a 'slot just taken' live update, skeleton states for >300ms waits, and aria-live regions all add JS and DOM. A 99 score achieved by stripping these makes the booking feel dead and INCREASES abandonment — a fast page nobody trusts to tap is not a win. The dangerous failure mode is a calendar that scores 99 because tapping a slot does a full Livewire round-trip with no local feedback, so the user double-taps, gets a 23P01 conflict, and bails.
tensions.reconciliation: Separate 'time-to-interactive cost' from 'time-to-FEEDBACK cost.' Press feedback, the hold timer, and slot selected/disabled state are pure Alpine + CSS transforms (transform/opacity only, already in the Livewire+Alpine core JS the perf lens budgeted at <=80KB) — they add zero network and ~0ms TBT because they run on already-parsed code. Only the authoritative availability check is a debounced Livewire round-trip, optimistically reflected in the UI first. CLS=0 is preserved by reserving the slot-grid box and timer box at SSR with fixed dimensions (tabular-nums for the countdown so it never reflows). This gives both lenses what they want: the route still scores 99 (no new render-blocking bytes, CLS=0, TBT<150ms) AND the booking feels alive. Bake a rule: 'every interactive affordance must be classified as client-instant (Alpine/CSS) or server-authoritative (Livewire); user feedback is always client-instant, truth is always server-authoritative.'

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer (strict CSP, OTP rate-limits, hard disclosures)
tensions.tension: Three security controls directly degrade UX if implemented naively. (1) Strict CSP with no 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval' is correct, but Alpine x-data inline expressions and Livewire's inline init can break the very client-side affordances I rely on for instant feedback — if security forces all interactivity behind nonce'd external bundles or kills Alpine inline, the UX-instant layer dies. (2) Layered OTP rate-limits and 'constant-time non-revealing responses' (anti-enumeration) mean the UI must NOT tell the user 'this number isn't registered' or 'too many tries' specifically — but vague errors violate my error-clarity + error-recovery rules and strand confused users. (3) The mandated 'hard, unmissable, prominent disclosure' of cancellation/refund/non-refundable-service-fee on EVERY transaction page is a conversion killer if it's a wall of red legal text blocking the pay button.
tensions.reconciliation: (1) CSP: Alpine 3 is CSP-compatible via the official @alpinejs/csp build (expressions in component objects, not inline strings) — adopt it from v1 so strict script-src 'self' 'nonce-...' holds AND instant client UX survives; verify in Lighthouse best-practices which also rewards strict CSP. (2) OTP errors: design a single, friendly, non-revealing copy that is still actionable — 'If this number is registered, we sent a code. Didn't get it? Resend in 0:30' — constant across enumeration outcomes, with a visible cooldown timer instead of a scary 'blocked' message. This satisfies anti-enumeration (same response either way) and my error-recovery rule (clear next step + cooldown). For genuine rate-limit lockout, show a calm 'Please try again in X minutes' with a WhatsApp-support fallback, never a dead end. (3) Disclosure: progressive disclosure, not suppression. Show a compact, scannable, always-visible summary chip ('Free cancel until 24h before · Service fee non-refundable') adjacent to the price, with a tap-to-expand full policy and a REQUIRED explicit checkbox/acknowledgement immediately above the pay button. This is legally 'hard and prominent' (acknowledged, logged) yet converts because it's structured, not a fear-wall. Use status color tokens, not alarm-red, for routine policy.

tensions.withLens: PWA + mobile-app packaging engineer (app-shell SPA, wire:navigate, deep links)
tensions.tension: The packaging lens wants the dashboard as a persistent app-shell SPA driven by wire:navigate with a static bottom tab nav so the chrome 'survives wrapping and feels native.' Two UX risks: (a) wire:navigate SPA navigation can silently drop scroll position, filter state, and form-in-progress on back — violating my state-preservation and back-stack-integrity rules — which is brutal in a dashboard where staff filter a long booking list then tab away. (b) A persistent bottom tab bar in a WebView/TWA can collide with the OS gesture bar and notch (safe-area), and if it exceeds 5 items or mixes primary nav with actions it breaks bottom-nav-limit and adaptive-navigation. Also 'feels native' is asserted but Apple's 4.2 minimum-functionality bar is really a UX bar: a wrapper that's just the website gets rejected because it offers nothing app-like.
tensions.reconciliation: (a) Mandate that wire:navigate is configured to preserve scroll and that filter/sort/date state lives in the URL query string (which also serves the perf lens's deep-link requirement and SEO-style shareable dashboard URLs) so back-navigation restores state for free and a refresh inside the wrapper never loses context. Persist in-progress forms (form-autosave rule) for any multi-field dashboard form. (b) Bottom tab nav: cap at 5 top-level destinations (Calendar, Bookings, Credits, Reviews, More), every item icon+label, active state highlighted, and wrap the whole shell in env(safe-area-inset-bottom) padding so it clears the gesture bar in TWA and Capacitor. (c) For Apple 4.2, the app-readiness deltas the packaging lens already lists (push notifications for new bookings, native share, install) are exactly the 'app-like' UX that clears 4.2 — so co-own them: I specify the UX (a real notifications inbox screen, pull-to-refresh on the calendar, offline 'last synced' state) that makes it pass review, not a thin wrapper.

tensions.withLens: UI / visual design-system architect
tensions.tension: Largely aligned — we both want tokens, 16px min inputs, tabular numerals, AA contrast. The real tension is DENSITY vs ACCESSIBILITY/TOUCH. The UI lens proposes a 'compact dashboard density mode' with 14px data text and tighter padding for 'operator throughput.' But compact padding can push touch targets below the 44x44pt minimum on the dashboard's most-tapped surface — the slot grid and booking rows — and 14px is fine for read-only data but risky if it's also the tap label. Their shallow elevation + small-radius system is good for perf but must still produce a visible focus ring (my focus-states rule, which the perf lens also needs for accessibility=99) and visibly distinct hover/active/disabled states; a too-subtle token set fails state-clarity. Secondly, dark mode 'gated behind a class toggle' is fine, but if dashboard staff work in dim venue back-offices (their own stated use case) and dark mode ships later, v1 light-only must still hold AA in low light.
tensions.reconciliation: Decouple visual density from touch density: 14px text and tight VISUAL padding are allowed, but every interactive element keeps a >=44x44pt HIT area via hit-slop/invisible padding even when the visible chip looks compact (the slot grid renders small but taps big). Make the focus-visible ring and the five booking-state colors (hold/paid/confirmed/cancelled/no_show) part of the mandatory token contract with verified AA contrast in BOTH densities, and require each state to carry an icon or text label, not color alone (color-not-only) — critical because color-blind staff and grayscale e-ink-like dim screens must still read 'hold' vs 'confirmed.' Agree dark-mode-ready tokens now; in the meantime hold AA at the lower end of typical back-office brightness. This keeps the CSS budget (shared tokens, one font) while satisfying touch + a11y.

finalRequirements: [must] Booking flow ergonomics + instant feedback: The discover->slot->pay flow must complete in minimal taps with feedback within 100ms of every tap. Slot press, selected/disabled state, and the hold countdown timer are client-instant (Alpine CSP-build + CSS transform/opacity, zero network); only availability truth and the hold are server-authoritative (debounced Livewire, optimistic UI). Reserve the slot-grid and timer boxes at SSR (fixed dims, tabular-nums) so CLS=0. On a 23P01 'slot just taken' conflict, show a calm inline message and auto-refresh the grid — never a raw error or a dead end.

[must] Touch, tap-target & safe-area compliance on BOTH surfaces: Every interactive element has a >=44x44pt hit area (use invisible hit-slop where the visible chip is compact, e.g. dashboard slot grid) with >=8px spacing; touch-action:manipulation to kill tap delay. All fixed chrome (public sticky CTA bar, dashboard bottom tab nav) padded with env(safe-area-inset-*) so it clears notch/Dynamic Island/gesture bar inside browser, TWA, and Capacitor. Bottom nav capped at 5 top-level items, icon+label, active state highlighted.

[must] Accessibility WCAG 2.2 AA as a Lighthouse-99 co-requirement: Visible focus-visible ring on every interactive element (tokenised, AA contrast in both densities); semantic landmarks + sequential headings; icon-only buttons get aria-label; booking-state and all functional color carry an icon/text label (never color alone); form errors via role=alert/aria-live; reduced-motion respected; system text-scaling (Dynamic Type) supported without truncation. Accessibility category must score >=99 on every route, same gate as performance.

[must] Form & input UX (the OTP/phone + payment path is the conversion choke point): Visible labels (not placeholder-only); inputmode/type tel for phone, numeric for OTP, with autocomplete=one-time-code so the OTP autofills from SMS/WhatsApp; >=16px font on all inputs (no iOS zoom-on-focus); inline validation on blur not keystroke; errors below the field with cause+fix; auto-focus the first invalid field; loading+disabled state on every async submit. OTP error copy is single, friendly, non-revealing AND actionable with a visible resend cooldown timer ('If registered, we sent a code · Resend in 0:30') — satisfies anti-enumeration without stranding users.

[must] Refund/cancellation disclosure as progressive disclosure, not a fear-wall: A compact, always-visible policy summary chip next to the price (status-color tokens, not alarm-red), tap-to-expand full venue policy, and a REQUIRED explicit acknowledgement control immediately above the pay button (acknowledgement logged for legal 'hard disclosure' + audit-trail). Satisfies the security/legal 'hard prominent disclosure on every transaction page' while preserving conversion through structure and scannability.

[must] State preservation, deep-linking & back integrity (web + wrapper): Filter/sort/date/tab state lives in the URL query string on both public discovery and the dashboard, so wire:navigate back-navigation, refresh-inside-wrapper, share, and the app's deep links all restore scroll + state losslessly (co-serves packaging deep-link + SEO shareable-URL needs). Multi-field dashboard forms auto-save drafts so an accidental wrapper dismissal never loses work.

[must] Trust & comfort at money-moments + loading/empty/offline states: Skeleton/shimmer (not blank spinners) for any wait >300ms; a clear booking-confirmation success state with what-happens-next (WhatsApp confirmation incoming); helpful empty states with a next action (no venues in radius -> widen radius / nearest cluster; empty credit wallet -> top-up CTA for venues); a visible 'last synced HH:MM' timestamp on stale-while-revalidate availability so users never act on stale data; calm offline fallback. Confirmation dialog before destructive/irreversible actions (cancel booking, issue refund) with undo where feasible.

[must] Two densities, one token contract — usability for both audiences: Airy public density for conversion/trust; compact dashboard density for operator throughput — but density is VISUAL only; touch density (>=44pt hit areas), AA contrast, focus rings, and the five booking-state tokens (hold/paid/confirmed/cancelled/no_show, each with icon+label) are identical and mandatory across both. Bahasa Indonesia copy, IDR with tabular figures, WIB times, locale-aware number/date formatting throughout. Plain-language microcopy (no jargon) — directly serves the owner's 'easy to use' requirement.
missedByOthers: No lens owns the COLD-START / EMPTY-INVENTORY UX, yet the spec's go-to-market explicitly hides discovery below a liquidity threshold and onboards only 3-8 anchor venues. UX must define what a player sees when radius search returns 1-2 venues or zero: 'show nearest cluster + WhatsApp the venue' fallback, never a blank or broken results page. This is a launch-blocking UX gap nobody else flagged.

Walk-in / staff entry speed is the dashboard's most-used real-world action (operators replacing WhatsApp+manual transfer) but no lens specifies its UX. It needs a one-screen, few-tap walk-in booking that runs through the same EXCLUDE constraint with instant conflict feedback — if it's slower than their current pen-and-paper, adoption fails regardless of Lighthouse or security.

Notification UX is treated by the packaging lens purely as a delivery/token plumbing problem, but the USER-facing side is unspecified: an in-app notifications inbox screen (new booking, low credit, refund request), and critically the permission-priming UX (never fire the native push prompt cold — prime it after the first successful booking). This also doubles as the 'app-like' functionality that clears Apple 4.2.

Hold-timer expiry UX is undefined: the spec sets a 10-minute hold and Redis lock, but nobody specifies what the player sees as it counts down, at T-2min, and on expiry. Without a visible, calm countdown + graceful 'your hold expired, here's the slot again if still free' recovery, users will rage at silently-lost slots — a direct trust/conversion hit at the money moment.

i18n copy ownership: the spec mandates Bahasa Indonesia primary with EN later, but no lens owns the writing of microcopy, error strings, OTP messages, and disclosures in plain Bahasa. 'Easy to use' lives or dies in this copy; it must be a tracked, reviewed asset (a string catalogue), not improvised in templates, and it must stay non-revealing where security requires.

crossOut.lens: UI / Design-System Architect — one self-hosted Tailwind v4 @theme token set driving two densities (airy public SSR + compact Livewire dashboard), engineered as a Lighthouse-99 constraint (sub-14KB critical CSS, zero CDN, one preloaded subset font, CLS=0).

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer (Lighthouse >=99 every route)
tensions.tension: The perf lens sets a hard 25KB critical-CSS inline budget (and 20KB deferred) per route. A genuine design system with two density modes, full component state matrices (default/hover/active/focus-visible/disabled/loading), a slot-grid, status colors, and dark-mode-ready tokens can easily blow past 25KB if every OKLCH token and both density variants ship to every public route. Worse: inlining critical CSS PER ROUTE (their requirement) fights my single-bundled-hashed-CSS governance model — per-route inlining can duplicate token declarations and defeats long-cache reuse of the shared sheet.
tensions.reconciliation: Split the design system physically along the route boundary, not the token boundary. (1) Define ALL tokens once in a @theme layer, but Tailwind v4 only emits a CSS variable for a token if it is actually referenced — so the public bundle, purged against only Blade/SSR templates, never ships dashboard-only slot-grid or compact-density utilities. Public critical CSS stays ~8-10KB; dashboard CSS is a SEPARATE purged bundle loaded only behind auth (its weight never touches a public Lighthouse run). (2) Resolve the per-route-vs-shared conflict with a two-tier delivery: inline a tiny route-specific critical slice (tokens + above-the-fold layout, <10KB) AND <link> the shared hashed component sheet with high cache priority — the inline slice covers first paint, the cached sheet is free on navigation 2+. (3) Gate dark-mode entirely behind a .dark class so its token block is emitted but adds ~0 render cost and never enters the public critical path. Net: my system makes their budget easier, not harder, because tokens collapse to shared CSS variables instead of repeated literal values.

tensions.withLens: Web performance engineer (CLS=0 on every route, font requirement)
tensions.tension: We BOTH demand CLS=0 and a self-hosted font, but a single brand font (Plus Jakarta Sans) is itself the #1 CLS risk: if the fallback metrics don't match, every text block reflows on swap. The perf lens says 'self-host a minimal subset font' but doesn't specify the fallback-metric contract — and `font-display:swap` (named in the spec 7.1) is precisely what CAUSES the swap-reflow they're trying to eliminate.
tensions.reconciliation: Make the metric-matched fallback a hard, shared deliverable owned by my lens and consumed by theirs: ship an @font-face with `size-adjust`, `ascent-override`, `descent-override`, `line-gap-override` computed to match Plus Jakarta Sans against a `local('...')`-pinned system stack (Roboto on Android/TWA, San Francisco on iOS/Capacitor) so the swap is metrically invisible -> CLS contribution = 0 even with `font-display:swap`. Better: prefer `font-display:optional` on the public LCP heading so a slow font NEVER swaps mid-paint (correcting spec 7.1's blanket `swap`). One preloaded variable subset woff2 (Latin + Bahasa diacritics + tabular figures), <=20KB, satisfies their font budget and my typography requirement simultaneously.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer (strict CSP, no unsafe-inline / unsafe-eval on script-src)
tensions.tension: A strict CSP that bans `unsafe-inline` is usually scoped to script-src, but teams over-rotate and also lock `style-src` to nonces/hashes. My design system relies on (a) inlined critical CSS (a <style> block), (b) Alpine's `x-bind:style` / dynamic style attributes for slot-grid states and the hold-timer, and (c) Tailwind-generated inline style hooks. A `style-src 'self'` with no `unsafe-inline` would break inline critical CSS and any dynamic style binding, hurting both CLS and the interactive booking UI.
tensions.reconciliation: Agree a CSP contract that keeps script-src strict but accommodates styles safely: (1) script-src uses nonces + strict-dynamic (Livewire 3 supports a CSP nonce; Alpine's CSP-safe build avoids eval) — I don't need inline scripts, so I fully support their strict script-src. (2) For styles, nonce the single inlined critical <style> block (style-src 'self' 'nonce-...') rather than allowing 'unsafe-inline' globally — the nonce is generated per-request and the inline critical CSS carries it. (3) Eliminate dynamic inline style attributes from my components: drive ALL slot/booking states with pre-defined token-based utility CLASSES toggled via Alpine (`:class`, not `:style`), and render the hold-timer as a width via a CSS custom property set through a class, so no element needs an inline style attribute. This lets style-src stay nonce-based with no 'unsafe-inline'. I will publish a 'CSP-safe component' rule in the design-system governance: components may toggle classes, never inline styles.

tensions.withLens: PWA + mobile-app packaging engineer (app-shell SPA via wire:navigate, native-feeling chrome)
tensions.tension: The packaging lens wants a persistent app-shell (static top bar + bottom tab nav, content swapped via wire:navigate) that 'feels native' and survives TWA/Capacitor. My component spec was written surface-agnostic, but a persistent shell needs things a normal web design system often omits: safe-area insets (notch / iOS home indicator), a bottom-nav component sized for thumb reach, 44px minimum tap targets, and a shell that does NOT repaint chrome on navigation — otherwise wire:navigate flashes and breaks the native illusion the perf+packaging lenses both want.
tensions.reconciliation: Extend the design system with an explicit 'app-shell' component layer that they consume: (1) add `env(safe-area-inset-*)` padding tokens to the spacing scale so the top bar and bottom nav respect notches in both TWA and Capacitor. (2) Specify a bottom-tab-nav primitive (>=56px height, >=44px tap targets, active/inactive token states) as a first-class component, not an afterthought. (3) Mark the shell chrome (top bar, bottom nav) with `wire:persist` / outside the wire:navigate swap region so tokens render once and never re-paint — this is a design-system structural rule, not just their concern. (4) Provide a single density-context attribute on the shell so the dashboard renders compact while public stays airy from the SAME components. This makes 'one codebase, two surfaces' real at the token+component level.

tensions.withLens: Application Security Engineer + product 'easy to use / conversion' (hard refund disclosures vs comfortable, low-friction UI)
tensions.tension: The spec mandates a 'hard, unmissable disclosure' on every transaction page (cancellation policy, service-fee-non-refundable, venue-executes-refunds). 'Unmissable' legal/compliance UI naturally trends toward loud warning boxes, dense red text, forced scroll — which directly fights my 'airy, trustworthy, comfortable, easy-to-use' mandate and can depress booking conversion. There's a real tension between legally hard and emotionally comfortable.
tensions.reconciliation: Design a dedicated, tokenized 'disclosure' component that is provably prominent WITHOUT being hostile: (1) use my semantic status tokens (a calm 'info/caution' color at AA contrast, not alarm-red) with generous padding so it reads as trustworthy guidance, not a warning. (2) Make it unmissable via PLACEMENT and a required affirmative interaction (a checkbox/'I understand the venue's policy' gate on the pay button) rather than via visual aggression — satisfies the security/legal 'hard disclosure' and keeps the page calm. (3) Standardize the cancellation-policy tiers as a compact, scannable table component (reusing the slot-grid/data-table tokens) so the actual policy is legible at a glance. Comfortable and compliant are reconcilable when prominence comes from structure + interaction, not from ugliness.

finalRequirements: [must] Single Tailwind v4 @theme token source of truth, two physical bundles: Define ALL design tokens (color in OKLCH, --text-* with baked line-height/letter-spacing/weight, 4/8pt spacing, radius, elevation, safe-area insets) once in an @theme layer. Build TWO purged/hashed CSS bundles from it — a public bundle (purged against Blade/SSR only) and a dashboard bundle (purged against Livewire components, loaded only behind auth) — so dashboard-only weight (slot-grid, compact density) never reaches a public Lighthouse run. Hard rule: every visual value in templates comes from a token; NO arbitrary values. Public critical CSS budget <=10KB inlined; total shared component sheet hashed + long-cached.

[must] Plus Jakarta Sans, one preloaded subset woff2 (<=20KB) with a metric-matched fallback contract that guarantees CLS=0: ship @font-face with size-adjust + ascent/descent/line-gap-override tuned against a local() system stack (Roboto/Android-TWA, SF/iOS-Capacitor). Use font-display:optional on the LCP heading (overriding the spec's blanket swap) and swap elsewhere; subset = Latin + Bahasa diacritics + tabular figures for IDR/time. This single deliverable satisfies the perf lens's font budget and CLS=0 simultaneously.

[must] Fixed modular type scale as --text-* tokens with line-height/letter-spacing/weight per step; dashboard data reads at 14px, public marketing larger; strict 16px minimum on ALL form inputs (prevents iOS zoom-on-focus in Safari/Capacitor and protects tap-target accessibility/Lighthouse). Tabular numerals enabled on all price/time/IDR contexts.

[must] 4px-base / 8px-rhythm spacing scale shared by both surfaces, with two documented density modes (airy public vs compact dashboard) achieved by SWAPPING component padding tokens via a single density-context attribute on the app-shell — not by forking stylesheets or inventing ad-hoc margins. Include env(safe-area-inset-*) padding tokens so the future TWA/Capacitor shell respects notches/home-indicator.

[must] Semantic OKLCH color token layer (no raw palette names in markup): AA-contrast-guaranteed text tokens, a court/sport accent, and explicit booking-state status tokens (hold/paid/confirmed/cancelled/no_show) reused across slot-grid, dashboard, and the disclosure component. Status colors chosen to be unambiguous AND calm (caution != alarm-red) so the mandatory refund disclosure can be prominent without hurting conversion.

[must] Core components as paired Blade (SSR public) + Livewire/Blade (dashboard) primitives that are visually identical via shared tokens — button, input, card, nav, modal — each with full state matrix (default/hover/active/focus-visible/disabled/loading) and a visible accessible focus ring (focus-visible). CSP-SAFE GOVERNANCE RULE: components toggle pre-defined token utility CLASSES via Alpine (:class), NEVER inline style attributes (:style), and the hold-timer renders via a CSS custom property set through a class — so the strict CSP can keep style-src on nonces with no 'unsafe-inline', and script-src stays strict (Alpine CSP build, Livewire nonce).

[must] Dedicated app-shell component layer (consumed by the PWA/packaging lens): persistent top bar + bottom-tab-nav (>=56px tall, >=44px tap targets, active/inactive token states) marked wire:persist / outside the wire:navigate swap region so chrome renders once and never repaints during in-app navigation — making 'feels native in TWA/Capacitor' a structural property of the design system.

[must] Token-driven slot-grid / calendar spec (the booking core): unambiguous slot states (available/selected/held/booked/disabled) using status tokens, >=44px thumb-tap targets on mobile, dense scannable layout on desktop via the density context, zero layout shift when slots refresh (reserve box; states are class swaps, not size changes). Same component powers public booking and dashboard calendar.

[must] Tokenized, compliant 'disclosure' component for the mandatory cancellation/refund disclosure: prominent via placement + a required affirmative gate on the pay button (not via aggressive color), AA-contrast caution tokens, and a compact scannable cancellation-policy tier table reusing data-table tokens. Reconciles 'hard disclosure' (security/legal) with 'comfortable/easy-to-use' (UX).
[should] Small radius set + deliberately shallow, cheap (single-layer) elevation tokens — Lighthouse-safe shadows that feel soft without paint-cost or 'AI-default' heaviness.

[nice] Dark-mode-ready tokens from day one (dashboard/dim back-office + future app), gated entirely behind a .dark class so the token block adds ~0 render cost and never enters the public Lighthouse-critical path; ship light-first in v1.
